proposals.title: Temporal Bootstrapping with LLM Priors for Robust Video Understanding on Small-Scale Datasets
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language benchmarks and models are designed for large, statistically representative datasets, yet many real-world video tasks (e.g., specialized medical procedures, rare event analysis) involve small, sparse sets where standard evaluation is unreliable. This mismatch limits the deployment of generalist AI in data-scarce domains. Addressing this gap is crucial for advancing robust, general-purpose video-language models beyond curated web-scale data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that injecting structured, temporally-aware priors from Large Language Models (LLMs) into a generative video-differencing framework can stabilize learning and evaluation on small video sets, making performance metrics statistically meaningful despite limited samples.
proposals.method: We will extend the Video Action Differencing framework by integrating stochastic injection techniques from distribution-to-distribution modeling to handle uncertainty. An LLM (e.g., GPT-4) will generate plausible temporal narrative variations and counterfactual descriptions for input video-text pairs, creating an augmented 'prior distribution'. This will be fused with the visual differencing pathway via a cross-attention module, allowing the model to learn from both limited real data and the statistically richer, language-guided synthetic variations.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel benchmark protocol for evaluating video-language models on small datasets, alongside a training/evaluation method that yields statistically robust metrics. It will demonstrate improved generalization for biomedical or other niche video tasks where data is scarce, directly advancing towards practical biomedical generalist AI.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its explicit fusion of LLM-generated temporal narratives with generative video differencing for small-set robustness, moving beyond simple data augmentation to distributional alignment guided by language priors.

proposals.title: HILAR: Human-in-the-Loop Action Refinement for Fine-Grained Video Difference Description
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models for video difference description, such as those built on CLIP and LLMs, often produce generic or semantically imprecise captions, limiting their utility in critical applications like instructional video analysis or biomedical procedure comparison. While benchmarks exist, they lack a mechanism for iterative, human-guided refinement to align model outputs with nuanced, task-specific human judgment. Integrating structured human feedback is essential to bridge this semantic gap and create more reliable, user-aligned video understanding systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a constrained, preference-based human feedback loop, integrated into a temporal video-differencing model, can iteratively refine generated descriptions to be more accurate, detailed, and aligned with human perception than a single forward pass.
proposals.method: We will extend the Video Action Differencing framework by incorporating a refinement module guided by human preferences. The method involves: 1) Generating initial difference descriptions using a frozen vision-language backbone (e.g., VideoCLIP + LLM). 2) Presenting multiple candidate descriptions (via stochastic injection in the LLM's decoding) to human annotators for pairwise preference feedback. 3) Training a lightweight reward model on this preference data to predict human judgment. 4) Fine-tuning the description generator using reinforcement learning (e.g., PPO) with the learned reward, enforcing temporal consistency in the refined outputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel human-in-the-loop pipeline for fine-grained video description refinement and a new dataset of human preferences on video difference captions. It will demonstrate how targeted human feedback can efficiently improve the precision and utility of automated video analysis systems.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard video captioning or VQA, this focuses on integrating direct human preference feedback specifically for the challenging task of temporal difference description, combining stochastic generation for diversity with a learned reward model for iterative refinement.

proposals.title: Beyond Categorization: A Temporal-Contrastive Framework for General Video Understanding
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models (VLMs) and video benchmarks are heavily biased towards classification tasks, treating visual understanding as a problem of assigning discrete labels. This narrow focus, evident in datasets like Kinetics, may hinder the development of models with genuine, general-purpose scene comprehension, such as reasoning about object permanence, causal relationships, and fine-grained spatiotemporal dynamics. A paradigm shift is needed to treat classification as a downstream application, not the core objective of representation learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a video representation model trained without explicit classification objectives, but instead on dense, language-grounded temporal contrastive and differencing tasks, will develop more general and transferable visual understanding capabilities, outperforming classification-pretrained models on complex, non-categorical video reasoning benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework. First, we will train a video encoder using a novel multi-granularity temporal contrastive loss on large-scale, narratively dense video-text pairs (e.g., derived from open-source scientific literature videos). This loss will enforce consistency between language descriptions and video clips at multiple temporal scales. Second, inspired by 'Video Action Differencing', we will incorporate a dedicated differencing module trained to predict state changes between frames, described in language by a frozen LLM. The final model will be a generative VLM, using the trained encoder and a pretrained LLM, evaluated on dynamic scene description, causal reasoning, and procedural forecasting.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a new training paradigm and benchmark for evaluating general video understanding divorced from classification, 2) empirical evidence on the limitations of classification-centric pretraining, and 3) a publicly released model demonstrating superior performance on compositional and temporal reasoning tasks in video.
proposals.novelty: The proposal fundamentally repositions classification as a specialized, potentially misleading subtask rather than the primary goal of visual learning. It uniquely combines large-scale language grounding with explicit temporal differencing objectives, moving beyond the dominant contrastive image-text or video-action classification paradigms of pre-2026 research.

proposals.title: Beyond Generalism: A Fine-Grained Diagnostic Benchmark for Vision-Language Models
proposals.motivation: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) like CLIP and LLaVA have demonstrated impressive zero-shot performance on broad object recognition tasks. However, their capability on fine-grained, domain-specific classification—critical for applications in science, medicine, and detailed scene understanding—remains poorly characterized. A systematic evaluation is needed to identify whether their latent knowledge stems from genuine visual-semantic understanding or superficial dataset biases.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that current VLMs will exhibit a significant performance drop on fine-grained classification tasks compared to coarse-grained tasks, and that this drop correlates more strongly with the visual similarity of sub-classes than with linguistic label complexity.
proposals.method: We will curate and adapt a suite of existing fine-grained datasets (e.g., Stanford Dogs, iNaturalist, specialized subsets from biomedical literature) into a unified VLM benchmark. We will evaluate a range of models (CLIP variants, BLIP-2, LLaVA) using zero-shot, few-shot, and linear probe protocols. Inspired by Zhang's prior work on stochastic injection and action differencing, we will design controlled 'distractor' sets and employ temporal differencing techniques on static images to isolate and measure the models' sensitivity to subtle, discriminative visual features versus textual priming.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive diagnostic benchmark for VLM fine-grained understanding, establishing clear performance baselines. It will produce a public toolkit to quantify the 'specialization gap' in generalist models and offer insights into failure modes, guiding future architecture design and training data curation for improved visual grounding.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing benchmarks that test broad capabilities, our approach diagnostically isolates the challenge of visual fine-grainedness from linguistic complexity. It uniquely adapts temporal 'differencing' concepts from video analysis to create controlled ablation studies on static image features.

proposals.title: Temporal In-Context Tuning: Eliciting Latent Video Understanding in Vision-Language Models via Few-Shot Prompting
proposals.motivation: Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) like CLIP and their video-capable successors (e.g., VideoCLIP) have shown impressive zero-shot capabilities, but their performance on complex, temporally-aware video tasks (e.g., fine-grained action recognition, procedural reasoning) often lags behind supervised models. Fine-tuning these models is resource-intensive and can degrade their generalizability. A method to better unlock their latent knowledge without retraining is needed, especially for video domains where labeled data is scarce.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the latent temporal and semantic knowledge of video-capable VLMs for complex classification tasks can be effectively and robustly elicited through structured few-shot prompting that explicitly models temporal relationships between in-context examples.
proposals.method: We will develop a prompting framework, Temporal In-Context Tuning (TICT), that builds on in-context learning principles from LLMs. For a target video task (e.g., from benchmarks like Something-Something or a new dataset derived from open literature), we will design a prompt template that sequentially presents K (few-shot) video-text example pairs, where the examples are strategically selected and ordered to highlight temporal variations or action differences, inspired by 'Video Action Differencing'. The model's frozen visual encoder and text encoder will process this context before making a prediction on a query video. We will systematically experiment with different stochastic injection strategies (inspired by Zhang's prior work on stochastic injection) for selecting and ordering the in-context examples to improve robustness and performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, training-free method to significantly improve video VLMs on complex temporal tasks, moving them closer to the performance of fine-tuned models. It will yield a systematic analysis of how in-context example selection and temporal structure affect VLM reasoning, contributing new insights to the field of efficient video model adaptation.
proposals.novelty: While in-context learning is established in NLP, its principled application to elicit *temporal understanding* in video VLMs is underexplored. The novelty lies in explicitly designing the temporal composition and stochastic selection of visual in-context examples, rather than treating them as independent shots, to guide the model's latent knowledge.

proposals.title: CLIP-Distill: Temporal Knowledge Distillation for Enhanced Video-Language Model Latent Alignment
proposals.motivation: Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) excel at generative tasks but often underperform specialized models like CLIP on fine-grained, discriminative video understanding. This gap limits their ability to ground language in precise visual semantics, a core requirement for tasks like temporal action localization and dense video captioning. Distilling CLIP's robust, contrastively-learned representation space into a generative VLM could significantly improve its latent space quality for decoding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distilling the frozen, discriminative knowledge of a CLIP model into a generative VLM via temporal feature alignment will improve the VLM's latent space structure, leading to superior performance on video-language tasks requiring precise visual-semantic decoding.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-stage knowledge distillation framework. First, we will extract spatio-temporal features from a frozen CLIP-ViT using a temporal adapter, inspired by Video Action Differencing techniques. Second, we will align these features with the intermediate representations of a generative VLM (e.g., a Flamingo or BLIP-2 variant) using a combination of contrastive and regression losses. The distillation will be performed on a large-scale video-language dataset, leveraging the structured noise injection methods from Zhang's prior work to regularize the feature transfer and prevent overfitting.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel distillation protocol specifically designed for temporal, generative VLMs, improving their discriminative capabilities without sacrificing generative fluency. It will provide a new benchmark for evaluating latent space alignment in video-language models and release a model with enhanced performance on tasks like zero-shot video classification and temporal grounding.
proposals.novelty: While distillation from CLIP to image models exists, applying it to temporally-aware generative VLMs for latent space refinement is unexplored. The novelty lies in the temporal adaptation of CLIP features and their structured distillation into a generative model's decoding pathway, moving beyond simple output mimicry.

proposals.title: ActiveCLIP-V: Efficient Video-Language Model Refinement via Temporal Uncertainty Sampling
proposals.motivation: Training and refining large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) like CLIP for video understanding requires massive, expensively labeled datasets. Current data collection is often passive and inefficient, failing to prioritize the most informative video-text pairs for model improvement. An active learning framework for VLMs would drastically reduce annotation costs and enable targeted enhancement of model capabilities, particularly for complex temporal reasoning tasks where data scarcity is acute.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an active learning strategy, which selects video clips based on a combination of visual-temporal uncertainty and semantic novelty measured by a large language model (LLM), will yield a more informative training subset than random or heuristic sampling, leading to superior VLM performance with fewer labeled examples.
proposals.method: We will extend the CLIP architecture with a temporal encoder (e.g., a lightweight VideoSwin) to create a baseline Video-CLIP model. The core active learning loop will use the model's predictive entropy over video-text alignments as the primary uncertainty measure. We will augment this with a novelty score derived from an LLM (e.g., GPT-4) analyzing the candidate clip's textual description against a history of previously selected samples. The acquisition function will be a weighted sum of uncertainty and novelty. Selected clips will be labeled via a human-in-the-loop interface, and the model will be iteratively fine-tuned.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, temporally-aware active learning framework for video-language data collection, 2) a new benchmark for evaluating active learning efficiency in video-text tasks, derived from existing datasets like HowTo100M, and 3) empirical evidence demonstrating significant efficiency gains in refining VLMs for action recognition and temporal grounding.
proposals.novelty: While active learning is established in image classification, its application to video-language models is nascent. Our approach uniquely integrates LLM-driven semantic novelty assessment with visual-temporal uncertainty, moving beyond simple confidence scores to select samples that are both hard for the model and semantically diverse for the task.

proposals.title: Minimal Data for Maximal Transfer: Determining the Per-Class Sample Efficiency Frontier for Vision-Language Model Classification
proposals.motivation: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) like CLIP are powerful zero-shot classifiers, but their performance is often surpassed by fine-tuning on downstream datasets, which requires significant labeled data. In resource-constrained domains like biomedicine, where Yuhui Zhang has prior experience, collecting large labeled datasets is expensive. Determining the minimal data required for a VLM to match or exceed CLIP's zero-shot accuracy would provide a crucial efficiency benchmark for practical deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a VLM, when fine-tuned with a carefully designed, minimal set of per-class examples and leveraging its pre-existing semantic knowledge, can achieve classification accuracy comparable to CLIP's zero-shot performance, and that this minimal data requirement can be characterized as a function of dataset and model properties.
proposals.method: We will conduct a systematic empirical study using established VLMs (e.g., CLIP, BLIP-2) and diverse classification benchmarks (e.g., ImageNet, specialized sets from scientific literature). The core method involves iterative, stratified sub-sampling to find the minimal per-class examples (n) needed for fine-tuned accuracy to converge to CLIP's zero-shot baseline. We will integrate Zhang's expertise in stochastic injection methods to augment these minimal subsets effectively and explore temporal/sequential data selection strategies inspired by 'Video Action Differencing' to identify the most informative samples. Analysis will correlate 'n' with dataset statistics (e.g., class granularity, texture vs. shape bias) and model scale.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic benchmark for the per-class sample efficiency of VLMs in classification, offering concrete guidance for data collection. It will contribute a methodology for data-efficient fine-tuning and a public resource of determined minimal datasets for common benchmarks, directly benefiting fields with data scarcity.
proposals.novelty: While few-shot learning is well-studied, explicitly framing the problem as finding the minimal data to match a strong zero-shot baseline (CLIP) provides a new, practical operational target. The integration of stochastic injection for robust augmentation on tiny data subsets and the application of temporal selection principles from video to static image sampling are novel methodological angles.

proposals.title: Exhaustive Class Exposure: Training Vision-Language Models from Scratch on a Dense, Hierarchical ImageNet-Scale Dataset
proposals.motivation: Current Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are often fine-tuned on large, web-crawled image-text pairs, which leads to uneven and unpredictable coverage of semantic categories, especially for fine-grained classes. This limits their reliability and structured understanding in zero-shot classification and open-vocabulary tasks. A foundational model trained from scratch on a dataset with exhaustive, high-frequency exposure to a comprehensive, hierarchical label space like ImageNet-22K could establish a more robust and uniformly knowledgeable visual concept system.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training a VLM architecture from random initialization on a dataset that provides exhaustive and balanced coverage of all ImageNet-22K classes will yield superior and more calibrated zero-shot generalization on fine-grained classification benchmarks compared to models trained on standard, noisy web data, by embedding a complete and structured semantic hierarchy directly into the model's foundational representations.
proposals.method: We will construct a novel dataset by programmatically generating multiple descriptive captions for every class in the ImageNet-22K hierarchy, pairing them with all available images per class. We will train a standard VLM architecture (e.g., a CLIP-style ViT-L/14 with a causal language model) from scratch on this data using a contrastive loss. Crucially, we will employ a temporally-aware, curriculum-based sampling strategy—inspired by Zhang's work on 'Video Action Differencing' and stochastic injection—to balance class frequency and progressively introduce hierarchical relationships and visual diversity, ensuring the model learns a stable and comprehensive concept space.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a new, publicly released large-scale dataset designed for exhaustive visual concept coverage; 2) a pre-trained VLM benchmarked to demonstrate state-of-the-art, calibrated performance on fine-grained zero-shot classification; and 3) an analysis of how structured, exhaustive training data shapes emergent properties in VLMs versus web-scale training.
proposals.novelty: While scaling datasets is common, the novelty lies in the deliberate, exhaustive construction of the training set to cover a complete, known ontology from the start, and in the application of temporal and stochastic curriculum strategies—drawn from the researcher's prior work—to the foundational training of a VLM, rather than to downstream video or generative tasks.

proposals.title: Minimalist Vision-Language Models: A Systematic Ablation Study for Efficient Video Classification
proposals.motivation: Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) like CLIP and their video extensions have become foundational for multimodal tasks, but their computational cost and architectural complexity hinder deployment in resource-constrained settings. While prior work focuses on scaling up, a systematic understanding of which components are essential for robust classification performance is lacking. This gap is critical for developing efficient, specialized models for real-time video understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant portion of a standard VLM's architecture is redundant for classification tasks, and that a minimal, task-specific model can be derived through systematic ablation without substantial performance loss.
proposals.method: We will perform a structured ablation study on a state-of-the-art video VLM (e.g., VideoCLIP, InternVideo) as a baseline. We will systematically remove or replace components, including: (1) the temporal aggregation module (e.g., mean-pooling vs. complex transformers), (2) the cross-modal attention layers, and (3) the depth and width of the visual and text encoders. Evaluation will be conducted on established video classification benchmarks (e.g., Kinetics, Something-Something v2) and the biomedical video-text dataset from Zhang's prior work, measuring accuracy, latency, and parameter count.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled blueprint for constructing efficient, high-performance VLMs for classification, moving beyond heuristic scaling. It will yield a set of minimal architectural templates and identify the core components necessary for temporal reasoning, directly advancing the field of efficient multimodal AI.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior model compression or distillation work, this study takes a first-principles, ablation-based approach to discover the minimal sufficient architecture for video classification within the VLM paradigm, explicitly linking architectural choices to temporal and cross-modal functionality.

proposals.title: Diagnosing Video-Language Model Failures via Embedding Space Geometry and Temporal Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Current methods for diagnosing failures in video-language models (VLMs) often rely on training auxiliary classifiers on labeled error data, which is expensive and task-specific. This creates a bottleneck for model understanding and debugging, especially for complex temporal tasks. A method to directly diagnose failures from the model's own embedding space would enable faster, more flexible analysis of model behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: The failure modes of a video-language model on a given task are directly encoded in the geometric structure and temporal evolution of its internal video and text embeddings, and can be diagnosed without training a separate classifier.
proposals.method: We will leverage a large-scale video-language benchmark (e.g., derived from Zhang's prior work on scientific video-language datasets) to analyze failure cases. For a given task (e.g., action recognition), we will extract video and text embeddings from a pre-trained VLM like VideoCLIP. We will then apply geometric analysis (e.g., measuring embedding dispersion, alignment angles between video and text, or using stochastic injection techniques from Zhang's prior work to probe distributional shifts) to identify signatures of model confusion. Temporal analysis will involve comparing the evolution of video embeddings for failure vs. success cases, inspired by 'Video Action Differencing' methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, efficient paradigm for model diagnosis that bypasses the need for error classifiers. It will yield a set of interpretable, geometry-based metrics for VLM failure analysis. The findings could inform more robust training objectives and evaluation benchmarks for video-language understanding.
proposals.novelty: While embedding analysis is common in vision and language, applying geometric and temporal diagnostics specifically to the joint video-text embedding space for failure analysis is novel. The approach inverts the standard pipeline by diagnosing from the model's intrinsic representations rather than an external learned function.

proposals.title: When Words Are Not Enough: Diagnosing Failure Modes of Language-Based Rectification in Video Understanding Models
proposals.motivation: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used to guide and correct vision-language models via textual feedback, a process known as language-based rectification. However, this paradigm assumes language is a sufficient medium for all corrective interventions. In video understanding, where temporal dynamics and fine-grained visual details are critical, this assumption may be flawed. Identifying the specific failure modes where visual data is necessary is crucial for building robust, generalist AI systems, especially in domains like biomedical video analysis where errors can be costly.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that language-based rectification fails systematically for video tasks requiring precise spatiotemporal localization or the disambiguation of visually similar but semantically distinct actions, and that targeted injection of visual evidence can rectify these failures where language alone cannot.
proposals.method: We will construct a diagnostic benchmark, building on principles from Video Action Differencing and large-scale datasets, to test video-language models (e.g., Video-CLIP variants) on tasks where language feedback is intentionally ambiguous or incomplete. We will use LLMs (e.g., GPT-4) to generate corrective textual prompts. For failure cases, we will implement a 'visual injection' module, inspired by stochastic injection techniques for generative modeling, which selectively integrates keyframe or short clip embeddings from the original video to augment the language guidance. The model's performance will be evaluated with and without this visual intervention.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a taxonomy of failure modes for language-only intervention in video understanding, a benchmark to test them, and a proof-of-concept method for hybrid language-visual rectification. It will advance the design of more robust multimodal AI by clarifying the distinct roles of language and vision in model guidance.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses language to guide models, this proposal systematically challenges its sufficiency for video, a critical gap. It uniquely combines diagnostic benchmarking with a novel visual injection mechanism, moving beyond purely textual feedback loops.

proposals.title: NoisyCLIP: Robust Video-Language Alignment via Stochastic Injection and Temporal Coherence
proposals.motivation: Current video-language models like CLIP rely on meticulously curated, high-quality caption datasets, which are expensive and scarce, limiting scalability. In contrast, web-scale video-text pairs are abundant but noisy, containing misalignments, incomplete descriptions, and irrelevant language. A method to effectively learn from this noisy data is crucial for scaling video understanding models. This directly addresses a core challenge in building robust, general-purpose vision-language AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling and injecting structured noise during training, a video-language model can learn robust temporal representations from uncurated, web-scale data, outperforming models trained on clean data alone or with simple data augmentation.
proposals.method: We will extend the Video Action Differencing framework and integrate principles from stochastic injection for generative modeling. A dual-stream architecture (vision transformer for video, language model for text) will be trained with a novel contrastive loss. Crucially, we will synthetically generate realistic caption noise (e.g., temporal misalignment, irrelevant keywords, paraphrasing) based on patterns mined from web data and inject it stochastically into the training pipeline. A temporal coherence module, inspired by action differencing, will enforce consistency across noisy variants of the same video clip to stabilize learning.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel training framework for noise-robust video-language alignment, 2) a new benchmark derived from open web data (extending prior work on scientific literature datasets) to evaluate model robustness to real-world caption noise, and 3) empirical evidence on the scaling laws for video-language pre-training with noisy data.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic, generative modeling of caption noise for video-language pre-training, moving beyond simple filtering or heuristics. Combining stochastic noise injection with temporal action differencing to learn from imperfect data is an unexplored direction for video-language models.

proposals.title: CLIP-Rectify: Automated Language-Based Rectification for Temporal Video Classifiers
proposals.motivation: Vision-language models like CLIP have shown that language can guide visual understanding, but this capability is not systematically used to correct errors in specialized video classifiers. Current video action recognition models, trained on fixed datasets, often fail on out-of-distribution or fine-grained examples, requiring costly manual relabeling or retraining. An automated, language-driven rectification pipeline could adapt these classifiers post-hoc, improving robustness and reducing annotation burden.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Large Language Model (LLM) can generate targeted, corrective natural language descriptions for classifier errors, and that these descriptions can be used by a vision-language model to automatically adjust the classifier's output distribution on-the-fly, improving its temporal action recognition accuracy.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage pipeline. First, an LLM (e.g., GPT-4) analyzes classifier failure cases (misclassified video clips) and, given a task description, generates corrective textual prompts. Second, we leverage a pre-trained vision-language model (e.g., VideoCLIP) as a 'rectification module.' Inspired by stochastic injection methods for distribution-to-distribution modeling, we will design a temporal fusion mechanism where the corrective language embeddings modulate the classifier's logits or intermediate features, effectively applying a language-guided shift in the output distribution. The pipeline will be evaluated on benchmarks derived from datasets like Kinetics and the researcher's prior work on Video Action Differencing.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, automated framework for language-based model correction, moving beyond static prompt engineering, 2) a new benchmark task for evaluating the rectification of temporal video classifiers using language, and 3) empirical evidence on the efficacy of LLM-generated corrections for improving vision model robustness.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in automating the entire correction loop—from error diagnosis via LLMs to application via vision-language fusion—specifically for temporal video tasks, and in formalizing language-based rectification as a controlled distribution shift problem, connecting it to stochastic injection techniques.

proposals.title: LINGUA-ACTIVE: Language-Guided Active Learning for Efficient Video Action Annotation
proposals.motivation: Annotating video data for temporal action understanding is prohibitively expensive and labor-intensive, creating a major bottleneck for model development. While large vision-language models (VLMs) and active learning offer separate paths to efficiency, their synergistic potential for video remains underexplored. This work addresses the critical need to minimize human annotation effort in video benchmarks without sacrificing model performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using a large language model (LLM) to define semantically coherent, language-based slices of a video dataset will significantly improve the sample efficiency of a subsequent visual active learning loop, leading to more effective annotation strategies than either approach alone.
proposals.method: We will first use an LLM (e.g., GPT-4) to analyze text queries or class descriptions and propose 'language slices'—clusters of potential hard or informative video instances based on semantic and temporal reasoning. These slices will seed a pool-based active learning system where a temporal video model (e.g., a Video Swin Transformer) selects the most uncertain samples from these pre-filtered pools for human annotation. We will integrate stochastic injection techniques, inspired by Zhang's prior work, to augment the visual features during training and improve model robustness on the sparsely labeled data.
proposals.contribution: This project will deliver a novel, hybrid framework for video annotation that demonstrably reduces labeling cost. We will contribute a new benchmark protocol for evaluating annotation efficiency in video action tasks and release code to facilitate future research in data-efficient video understanding.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the tight, sequential coupling of LLM-driven semantic slicing with visual active learning, specifically tailored for the temporal structure of video, moving beyond static image tasks or using language models merely as labelers.

proposals.title: VidLanDiff: Video-Language Adaptation via Stochastic Injection and Temporal Coherence for Low-Resource Languages
proposals.motivation: Current large-scale vision-language models (VLMs) are predominantly trained on English-centric datasets, creating a significant performance gap for low-resource languages. This limits the global applicability of video understanding and generation benchmarks. Adapting these models effectively without extensive parallel video-text data is a critical, unsolved challenge in equitable AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a method combining stochastic injection techniques for robust cross-lingual feature alignment with temporal differencing for data-efficient video representation learning can enable effective video-language model adaptation to low-resource languages using only minimal parallel data.
proposals.method: We will adapt a pre-trained English VLM (e.g., CLIP or VideoCLIP) by fine-tuning it on a small, curated parallel corpus of videos with captions in a target low-resource language. Inspired by Zhang's prior work, we will employ controlled stochastic noise injection into the text and visual encoders during training to improve robustness and distribution matching. To leverage the temporal dimension efficiently, we will incorporate a Video Action Differencing-inspired auxiliary task that predicts action changes between video clips, encouraging the model to learn language-agnostic, temporally grounded features that require less linguistic supervision.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel adaptation framework for low-resource video-language tasks, 2) a new benchmark for evaluating video understanding in a selected low-resource language, and 3) empirical evidence on the synergy between stochastic regularization and temporal self-supervision for data-efficient multi-modal transfer.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of stochastic injection methods from generative modeling with temporal video differencing specifically for the under-explored problem of cross-lingual video-language adaptation, moving beyond static image-text alignment.

proposals.title: Dual-VAD: Vision-Aided Diagnostic Query Generation for Video Action Differencing
proposals.motivation: Current video action recognition and differencing models, while powerful, often fail in deployment due to subtle, systematic visual errors that are difficult to diagnose from scalar metrics alone. Debugging these failures requires manually inspecting failure cases and formulating targeted queries, a slow and expert-dependent process. Automating the generation of diagnostic language from visual error patterns would accelerate model refinement and deployment in critical applications like healthcare and autonomous systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dual-encoder architecture, trained to map visual error patterns in video action sequences to diagnostic natural language queries, can generate precise, actionable descriptions of model failure modes, thereby bridging the gap between vision model outputs and human-interpretable debugging.
proposals.method: We will build upon the Video Action Differencing framework to extract spatio-temporal error patterns. A frozen CLIP visual encoder will process these error clips, while a separate temporal encoder (e.g., a Video Swin Transformer) will capture error dynamics. The encoded features will be fused and fed into a pre-trained, instruction-tuned Large Language Model (LLM) like LLaMA or Vicuna, using a lightweight adapter (e.g., LoRA) to generate diagnostic queries. Training will use a novel dataset pairing synthetic and real video action errors with human-annotated diagnostic questions, applying stochastic injection techniques from prior work to improve robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel benchmark for vision-to-diagnostic-language generation, extending video-language datasets into the debugging domain; 2) the Dual-VAD model, a new tool for automated vision model diagnostics; and 3) empirical evidence on the utility of generated queries for guiding model improvement cycles.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct translation of visual error *dynamics* into diagnostic language, moving beyond static image captioning or generic video description, and in its specific application to the debugging loop for temporal vision models.

proposals.title: Temporal-Aware Task Specialization: Improving Video Action Differencing with Compact, Domain-Focused Models
proposals.motivation: Large-scale vision-language models (VLMs) and LLMs, while powerful, often exhibit suboptimal performance on specific, fine-grained temporal reasoning tasks like video action differencing, where the goal is to identify subtle changes between video sequences. The pursuit of generalist AI can lead to models that are computationally expensive and lack precision on niche benchmarks. There is a clear need for strategies that leverage smaller, more efficient models to achieve superior performance on well-defined tasks where large models fail.
proposals.hypothesis: A compact, task-specialized model, trained with explicit temporal injection mechanisms and focused on the specific distribution of a video action differencing benchmark, can outperform a larger, generalist VLM on that task by avoiding the representational and attentional dilution inherent in broad pre-training.
proposals.method: We will adapt the 'stochastic injection' framework from Zhang's prior work, specifically applying distribution-to-distribution injection techniques to enforce robust temporal feature learning in a small-scale encoder-decoder architecture. The model will be trained and evaluated on a curated benchmark derived from existing video datasets (e.g., Something-Something, Epic-Kitchens) formatted for action differencing. We will systematically compare this specialized model against frozen and fine-tuned versions of large VLMs (e.g., CLIP-ViL, Flamingo) on temporal granularity, robustness to distractors, and inference cost.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete methodology for identifying 'failure tasks' of large models and a recipe for building superior, cost-effective specialized alternatives. It will yield a new, publicly available benchmark for evaluating temporal action differencing and a compact model that sets a new state-of-the-art on this specific task, challenging the assumption that scale is always preferable.
proposals.novelty: The approach moves beyond simple model compression or fine-tuning; it strategically uses stochastic injection from a prior generative modeling context to craft a specialized model from the ground up for a temporal task where large models are known to be weak, formalizing the concept of 'strategic downsizing' for performance gain.

proposals.title: Scaling Laws for Temporal Reasoning: Identifying Super-Linear Performance Gains in Video-Language Tasks
proposals.motivation: Scaling laws for language models typically show predictable, power-law performance gains. However, recent work suggests certain multimodal or reasoning tasks may exhibit 'emergent' abilities with sharper scaling curves. Identifying tasks where performance accelerates with scale, rather than saturating, is crucial for efficiently directing future compute and architectural innovation in video-language AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that complex, compositional video-language tasks requiring deep temporal reasoning and causal inference will exhibit super-linear scaling behavior with increased model scale (parameters, data, compute), as sufficient capacity unlocks the integration of disparate spatio-temporal and linguistic concepts.
proposals.method: We will construct a diagnostic benchmark of progressively complex video tasks, from simple action recognition to causal 'why' and counterfactual 'what if' questions, derived from structured datasets like Ego4D and our prior work on Video Action Differencing. We will scale a unified Vision-Language Model (VLM) architecture, building on models like Flamingo or VideoCLIP, across a controlled compute axis. Performance will be measured against a novel metric quantifying the acceleration of gains (second derivative of error vs. scale), with analysis informed by techniques from stochastic injection to probe model uncertainty and robustness during scaling.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) A validated taxonomy of video-language tasks by their scaling regime, 2) Evidence for or against the existence of super-linear scaling in temporal reasoning, guiding resource allocation for next-generation VLMs, and 3) A public benchmark focused on scaling-phase transitions for the community.
proposals.novelty: While scaling laws are well-studied in NLP and static vision, a systematic investigation into *accelerating* gains for temporal tasks is novel. Our approach uniquely combines diagnostic benchmark design with controlled scaling experiments specifically for video-language models, moving beyond mere observation to prediction of scaling phase changes.

proposals.title: Isomorphic Data Probes: Causal Attribution of Cross-Modal Performance Gaps to Data Structure vs. Optimization
proposals.motivation: Vision-language models exhibit a persistent 'modality gap' where performance differs across modalities for semantically aligned tasks. Pre-2026, it is unclear whether this gap stems from inherent statistical differences in data structures (e.g., images vs. text) or from suboptimal, modality-biased training protocols. Disentangling these causal factors is crucial for designing truly unified multimodal architectures and understanding the fundamental limits of cross-modal transfer.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that when a single, powerful, modality-agnostic optimization protocol is applied to perfectly semantically isomorphic data streams, residual performance differences can be causally attributed to the fundamental statistical structure of each modality, providing a quantifiable lower bound for the semantic divergence gap.
proposals.method: We will construct a synthetic dataset where identical semantic concepts (e.g., objects, actions, relations) are expressed natively in image, text, and audio sequences, ensuring perfect alignment. A unified transformer or Perceiver IO architecture will be trained on each modality stream separately using an identical, rigorously controlled optimization protocol (e.g., same loss, hyperparameters, and compute budget). We will analyze the residual performance gaps on held-out tasks (e.g., retrieval, generation, reasoning) after this optimal protocol alignment, using causal mediation analysis to attribute variance to data structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first causal, controlled measurement of the intrinsic 'data-structural' component of the modality gap, separating it from optimization artifacts. It will yield a diagnostic benchmark for evaluating the expressivity of unified architectures and inform the design of more effective modality-invariant representations.
proposals.novelty: While prior work compares modalities on real-world data with differing protocols, our method's novelty lies in the creation of a perfectly semantically aligned, multi-modal synthetic ecosystem to isolate the causal effect of data structure, an experimental design not previously implemented at scale.

proposals.title: Diagnosing Semantic Divergence in Multimodal Models: A Data-Centric Resolution Test
proposals.motivation: Current multimodal models, especially vision-language models, exhibit persistent performance gaps between modalities, often attributed to optimization challenges. However, this overlooks the fundamental role of data geometry. Drawing from pre-2026 work on benchmarks (e.g., CLIP) and temporal video analysis, we propose that the core issue is an ill-posed inverse problem: modalities are imperfect sensors of a shared semantic reality, and their data manifolds may have incomplete overlap.
proposals.hypothesis: The irreducible performance gap in multimodal alignment is primarily caused by the intrinsic geometric divergence and incomplete coverage of the underlying data manifolds for each modality, not by suboptimal model architectures or training algorithms.
proposals.method: We will create a synthetic, controlled environment where the 'true' aligned semantic space is known (e.g., a generative model producing perfectly paired video-text sequences). Using a fixed, near-optimal architecture (e.g., a ViT-LLM fusion model), we will systematically degrade the geometric coverage of one modality's data (e.g., by applying structured noise, masking, or subsampling akin to 'stochastic injection' from prior work) while holding the other modality's data ideal. We will then measure the residual alignment gap after extensive training, adapting geophysical 'checkerboard tests' to diagnose which semantic regions are unrecoverable due to data limitations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel diagnostic framework to quantify the data-induced, causal component of multimodal divergence. It will shift the field's focus from purely architectural solutions to data-centric analysis, offering principled metrics to guide dataset curation and model evaluation, particularly for complex tasks like video-language understanding.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in adapting geophysical resolution testing methodology to AI, framing modality gap as a data coverage problem rather than an optimization failure, and creating controlled experiments to isolate this causal factor—a perspective not explored in existing vision-language or video action differencing literature.

proposals.title: CausalGraph-MM: A Causal Intervention Framework for Diagnosing Representational Bottlenecks in Multi-Modal Vision-Language-Video Models
proposals.motivation: Multi-modal models combining vision, language, and video are central to generalist AI, but their performance is often limited by the 'modality gap'—a misalignment in the shared embedding space. Pre-2026 work primarily measures pairwise distances (e.g., CLIP-space gaps) but lacks a causal, system-level understanding of how discrepancies in one modality propagate and create bottlenecks for others, especially in three-plus modality settings common in video understanding and biomedical AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that representational influence between modalities forms an asymmetric, directed graph, where systematic interventions on specific encoders will reveal dominant 'anchor' modalities and critical bottlenecks that disproportionately harm downstream task performance.
proposals.method: We will extend a pre-trained multi-modal encoder (e.g., a Video-CLIP or biomedical VL model) and perform targeted interventions during inference and fine-tuning. These include stochastically injecting noise (inspired by Zhang's prior work) or ablating specific modality pathways. We will measure the causal effect on the representations of other modalities and on task performance (e.g., action differencing, VQA). Using causal discovery algorithms (e.g., PC algorithm) on the intervention data, we will construct a directed graph of representational influence.
proposals.contribution: This will yield a principled diagnostic tool to rank modality gaps by their downstream impact, moving beyond correlation to causation. The resulting methodology will provide concrete guidance for architecture design (e.g., where to add adapters), loss re-weighting, and data curation to efficiently close the most harmful gaps in complex multi-modal systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work quantifies the modality gap statically, this is the first to apply a causal intervention framework to diagnose the dynamic, directional influence structure between three or more modalities, directly linking specific representational failures to performance bottlenecks.

proposals.title: Meta-Initialization for Task-Adaptive Modality Alignment in Video-Language Models
proposals.motivation: In multimodal learning, the 'modality gap'—the geometric separation of representations from different modalities—is critical for downstream task performance. Pre-2026 work shows a controlled gap can improve robustness and compositionality, but existing methods prescribe a fixed, hand-designed gap that is brittle and fails to generalize across diverse tasks and data distributions. This limits the adaptability of models like CLIP and its video extensions to specialized benchmarks in action recognition or biomedical video-language understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learned initialization policy, optimized over a distribution of related multimodal tasks, will produce base model parameters that, after standard fine-tuning, converge to a task-optimal modality gap, leading to superior and more robust generalization compared to fixed-gap or standard pre-training strategies.
proposals.method: We will develop a meta-optimization framework using MAML or Reptile. The base model is a dual-encoder video-language architecture (e.g., VideoCLIP). The meta-learner is trained on a curated distribution of tasks from existing benchmarks (e.g., action differencing, biomedical QA). Each meta-task involves fine-tuning the base model from the current initialization. The meta-objective explicitly optimizes for post-fine-tuning performance and a robust gap metric (e.g., based on cross-modal retrieval accuracy under adversarial perturbation). The output is a learned initialization, not a fixed gap rule.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) A practical, learned initialization scheme for training more adaptable and robust video-language models. 2) Empirical evidence linking meta-learned initial conditions to emergent, beneficial geometric properties in representation space. 3) A new perspective on modality alignment as an optimization-to-be-adapted rather than a static constraint.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been used for fast adaptation, applying it explicitly to shape the emergent geometry of multimodal representations for gap control is novel. It moves beyond prescribing gap properties to learning how to best initialize a model so it naturally develops them during task-specific tuning.

proposals.title: Regularized Optimal Transport for Robust Video-Language Alignment
proposals.motivation: Cross-modal alignment between video and language is a core challenge for multimodal AI, essential for tasks like video captioning and retrieval. Standard contrastive or OT-based methods often struggle with the high-dimensional sparsity and inherent noise in video-text embeddings, leading to brittle and poorly generalizing models. A principled, robust alignment method is needed to improve the reliability of video-language systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating entropic or Lipschitz regularization into the optimal transport objective will yield a smoother, more stable transport plan between video and text embedding distributions, significantly improving alignment robustness and downstream task performance compared to unregularized OT or standard contrastive losses.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Sinkhorn algorithm for entropy-regularized OT and the dual formulation for Lipschitz-regularized OT (via a gradient penalty) to align features from a pre-trained video encoder (e.g., CLIP-ViT) and a language model. The transport cost will be defined on a joint embedding space. We will integrate this regularized OT loss as a training objective for fine-tuning on video-language benchmarks (e.g., MSR-VTT, ActivityNet Captions), evaluating its impact on retrieval and captioning metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, theoretically grounded alignment loss for video-language modeling that is demonstrably more robust to embedding noise. It will advance the state-of-the-art in cross-modal retrieval and generation by offering a stable alternative to standard contrastive learning. The research will also deliver a reusable, modular alignment module for the multimodal community.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic adaptation of regularized OT—inspired by robust mappings in computational hydrology—to the specific problem of video-language alignment, moving beyond standard OT or contrastive approaches used in prior vision-language work. This explicit focus on robustness via regularization for high-dimensional, sequential video data is a distinct contribution.

proposals.title: Federated Unsupervised Domain Adaptation for Modular Video-Language Alignment
proposals.motivation: Large-scale multimodal models like CLIP require centralized, monolithic training on massive, often private datasets, creating privacy, scalability, and data silo challenges. Federated learning offers a privacy-preserving alternative but struggles with the complex distribution shifts between modalities (e.g., video and language) and across decentralized clients. A modular, federated approach to domain adaptation is needed to align heterogeneous data sources without centralization.
proposals.hypothesis: Specialized, client-side vision and language modules can be aligned into a shared latent space through a federated adversarial domain adaptation objective, enabling effective multimodal understanding without sharing raw data or training a single large model.
proposals.method: We propose a modular architecture with separate, lightweight encoders for video (e.g., a temporal transformer) and text (e.g., a small LLM or BERT variant). A central server hosts a shared alignment module. Using principles from unsupervised domain adaptation (e.g., adversarial training with a gradient reversal layer or Maximum Mean Discrepancy minimization), client-side modules will learn to project features into a domain-invariant space. Federated averaging will synchronize the alignment module, while client-specific encoders adapt to local data distributions, drawing on Zhang's prior work in stochastic injection and video action differencing.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel framework for privacy-aware, scalable multimodal learning, a new benchmark for federated video-language alignment, and empirical evidence that collaborative adaptation of specialized modules can rival monolithic model performance on tasks like retrieval and classification.
proposals.novelty: It uniquely combines federated learning with adversarial domain adaptation for cross-modal (not just cross-domain) alignment, moving beyond monolithic models to a composable, privacy-by-design system for multimodal AI.

proposals.title: The Scaling Law of the Modality Gap: A Systematic Study of Alignment in Massively-Scaled Vision-Language Models
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has identified a 'modality gap' in contrastively trained vision-language models like CLIP, where image and text embeddings form separate clusters, potentially hindering cross-modal understanding and generative tasks. As models scale to hundreds of billions of parameters (e.g., GPT-4, PaLM-E), it is unknown whether this gap persists, widens, or transforms, fundamentally impacting the design and capabilities of next-generation multi-modal generalist AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the modality gap does not vanish with scale but instead evolves in a predictable, non-linear manner, and that its structural properties (e.g., relative curvature, density) become a key bottleneck for emergent cross-modal reasoning in models with over 100B parameters.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled scaling study using the OpenCLIP framework, training models from 100M to ~10B parameters on a massive, temporally-aligned video-text dataset (extending principles from Video Action Differencing). We will quantitatively characterize the gap using metrics from distribution-to-distribution modeling (inspired by Zhang's stochastic injection work) and topological data analysis. We will then probe how the gap affects downstream task performance (using established benchmarks) and test intervention strategies, including novel stochastic alignment losses and adaptive projection layers informed by the gap's geometry.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical scaling law for multi-modal representation alignment, offering concrete guidance for architecting future giant models. It will deliver a new diagnostic benchmark suite for evaluating the modality gap and propose novel training techniques to mitigate its negative effects at scale.
proposals.novelty: While the modality gap is known in small models, its systematic study across orders of magnitude in model scale is novel. Combining scaling laws with geometric analysis of the gap and testing interventions derived from generative modeling principles represents a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: VidReasoner: A Video-Centric Agent for Complex Spatio-Temporal Task Planning
proposals.motivation: Current LLM-based agents struggle with tasks requiring deep, fine-grained spatio-temporal reasoning over video, as they treat visual inputs as secondary, static context. This limits their effectiveness in real-world applications like robotics, video understanding, and interactive AI assistants. A specialized model that inherently reasons over temporal dynamics and visual details is needed to move beyond the LLM's language-centric bottleneck.
proposals.hypothesis: A video-centric reasoning model, built upon a temporally-aware vision backbone and trained for sequential decision-making, will outperform LLM-based agents on complex, long-horizon tasks defined in video or multimodal environments.
proposals.method: We will develop VidReasoner, an agent architecture with a Video Action Differencing (VAD) transformer as its core perceptual encoder to capture critical temporal state changes. This will be integrated with a lightweight, causal decision transformer head for action planning. The model will be trained on a novel benchmark derived from open video-language datasets (inspired by Zhang's prior work on biomedical literature) and procedurally generated environments, using a hybrid objective combining next-action prediction and temporal contrastive learning for robust state representation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a new state-of-the-art model for video-grounded agent tasks, 2) a rigorous benchmark for evaluating multimodal agent reasoning beyond static QA, and 3) empirical evidence for the limitations of LLMs as central agents in dynamic visual domains.
proposals.novelty: Instead of merely augmenting an LLM with a video encoder, we propose a fundamentally different agent paradigm where temporal visual reasoning is the primary mechanism, challenging the established LLM-as-brain design. The integration of stochastic injection techniques (from Zhang's prior work) during training will enhance the model's robustness to perceptual uncertainty.

proposals.title: V-Adapt: Dynamic In-Context Adaptation of Vision-Language Models for Interactive Video Understanding
proposals.motivation: Current video AI agents rely on static, pre-trained foundation models (e.g., VideoCLIP, Video-LLaMA) which are frozen after initial training. This rigidity limits their ability to adapt to novel tasks, specific video domains, or user feedback during an interactive session, a critical shortcoming for real-world applications like assistive robotics or interactive tutoring. The field lacks a principled framework for lightweight, dynamic model adaptation within a single episode of agent-environment interaction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a video agent equipped with a mechanism for dynamic, in-context parameter adaptation—using a combination of temporal feature differencing and stochastic injection—can significantly outperform static models on interactive video understanding benchmarks by continuously specializing to the task-at-hand.
proposals.method: We will develop V-Adapt, an agent framework built on a pre-trained vision-language model (e.g., CLIP-ViL). A lightweight 'Adapter Controller' network, informed by a temporal differencing module (inspired by Video Action Differencing), will generate context-aware parameter shifts (e.g., soft prompts or low-rank updates) based on the recent interaction history. We will employ controlled stochastic injection into these adaptation pathways, drawing from Zhang's prior work on distribution-to-distribution modeling, to improve robustness and prevent catastrophic forgetting during the live session. The system will be trained and evaluated on a curated subset of interactive tasks derived from existing video-language benchmarks (e.g., NExT-QA, Ego4D).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel technical framework for dynamic in-context adaptation of vision-language models for video, 2) a new interactive video benchmark focusing on adaptation efficiency, and 3) empirical evidence that controlled, stochastic adaptation during inference enhances agent performance and flexibility, advancing towards more generalist biomedical and embodied AI systems.
proposals.novelty: While fine-tuning and prompt-tuning exist, our approach uniquely focuses on *episodic*, *inference-time* adaptation driven by real-time video interaction history and stochastic regularization, moving beyond static models or adaptation methods that require full offline retraining.

proposals.title: UniFuse: A Fully Joint Architecture for Coherent Video-Language Reasoning via Early Token Fusion
proposals.motivation: Current state-of-the-art video-language models (pre-2026) often process visual and textual streams separately before late-stage fusion, which can limit deep, fine-grained cross-modal reasoning. This is a critical bottleneck for complex tasks requiring precise temporal and semantic alignment, such as detailed video question answering or procedural reasoning. A model that reasons over a unified representation from the outset could better capture the intricate dependencies between visual events and descriptive language.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a transformer-based architecture designed to process a single, fused sequence of visual and textual tokens from the initial layers will achieve superior performance on tasks requiring dense, temporally-grounded video-language understanding compared to models with separate encoders and late fusion.
proposals.method: We will design UniFuse, a transformer encoder that accepts a temporally ordered sequence of fused tokens. Video frames will be processed by a pre-trained, frozen visual encoder (e.g., CLIP-ViT) to extract spatio-temporal patch tokens. These will be interleaved with textual token embeddings from a language model within the input sequence, using learned modality-type embeddings. The core innovation is a 'Temporal Co-Attention' module within the early transformer blocks, which explicitly models pairwise attention between all visual and textual tokens while respecting their temporal order, enabling the model to perform joint reasoning from the start. Training will leverage large-scale video-text datasets (e.g., HowTo100M) and benchmark on tasks from Zhang's prior work like Video Action Differencing and established VL benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, fully joint architecture for video-language processing, 2) empirical evidence on the benefits of early fusion for temporal reasoning tasks, and 3) a new strong baseline model for video-language understanding, advancing towards more generalist multimodal AI. The findings will inform the design of future biomedical and general-purpose vision-language systems.
proposals.novelty: While models like Flamingo or VideoCoCa use cross-attention, they maintain separate encoders. UniFuse's novelty lies in its commitment to a single, fused token sequence from input, combined with a temporally-aware co-attention mechanism designed explicitly for the dense, sequential nature of video-language data, moving beyond the prevalent 'separate-then-fuse' paradigm.

proposals.title: Temporally-Aware Chain-of-Thought Planning for Vision-Language Tool Use in Video Understanding
proposals.motivation: Large Language Model (LLM) agents equipped with vision-language tools (e.g., CLIP, video captioners, action classifiers) show promise for complex video understanding. However, their planning strategies remain simplistic, often failing to sequence tool calls effectively over time or reason about temporal dependencies between observations. This limits their ability to solve tasks requiring multi-step, temporally-grounded reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating explicit temporal reasoning and structured world-state tracking into the LLM's planning module will significantly improve the efficiency and accuracy of its tool-use for video-based tasks.
proposals.method: We propose a novel planning architecture where an LLM planner interacts with a persistent temporal memory module. This module maintains a structured, timestamped history of tool-derived observations (e.g., object states, actions). The planner will be trained using reinforcement learning with a sparse success reward, guided by a learned temporal critic network that evaluates the coherence of sequential observations. We will benchmark this on video QA and procedural forecasting tasks derived from existing datasets, requiring chained tool use (e.g., Video Action Differencing followed by CLIP-based retrieval).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a new benchmark for evaluating LLM-based planning with vision-language tools on temporal tasks, and 2) an open-source framework with a novel planning algorithm that explicitly models temporal state for tool integration. It will advance the development of more capable and efficient generalist AI agents for video analysis.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on tool-augmented LLMs that treat tool calls as isolated steps, our method explicitly models the evolving temporal state of the video as a core component of the planning process, creating a feedback loop between past observations and future tool selection.

proposals.title: Dual-Stream Temporal Grounding: Enhancing Long-Form Language Reasoning with Video-Derived Event Structure
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models primarily use language as the query to retrieve or describe visual content, treating video as a passive data source. This limits the potential for rich, temporally structured visual information to actively shape and improve complex textual reasoning, such as narrative generation, procedural planning, or causal explanation. Inverting this paradigm could unlock more grounded and coherent long-form language generation by leveraging the explicit event dynamics and state transitions inherent in video.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model trained to extract and encode high-level, abstract event structures from video can provide a superior scaffolding mechanism for a Large Language Model (LLM), leading to more temporally consistent, factually grounded, and logically coherent long-form textual reasoning compared to text-only or standard vision-language prompting.
proposals.method: We propose a dual-stream architecture. The first stream is a temporal video encoder (building on methods like Video Action Differencing) trained to output a sequence of discrete, abstract event tokens representing key state changes and actions. The second stream is a pre-trained LLM. We will develop a novel cross-attention-based fusion module where the event token sequence acts as a continuous, dynamic prompt to guide the LLM's text generation. Training will involve a new benchmark derived from existing video captioning and procedural text datasets, with a focus on evaluating long-form reasoning (e.g., predicting subsequent steps, explaining causes).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel model architecture that inverts the standard video-language relationship, using video as an active guide for text generation; 2) a new benchmark and evaluation protocol for assessing video-guided long-form language reasoning; and 3) empirical evidence for the utility of explicit temporal structure from vision in improving textual coherence and grounding.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the inversion of the dominant 'language-queries-video' paradigm to 'video-guides-language,' specifically for long-form reasoning tasks. This moves beyond using video for simple description or classification, instead treating its temporal structure as a foundational scaffold for language models.

proposals.title: Poetic VQA: A Temporal Chain-of-Thought Framework for Generating Poetic Descriptions from Video
proposals.motivation: Current AI-assisted creative writing tools often rely on static image captions, which lack the nuanced, interpretive, and temporally evolving descriptions that inspire rich poetry. Grounded in pre-2026 vision-language research, this project addresses the gap between visual perception and creative language generation by leveraging the structured reasoning of Visual Question Answering (VQA) to extract deeper semantic and affective cues from visual media. This is crucial for building AI systems that can serve as more sophisticated muses for artistic endeavors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured, multi-stage VQA interrogation of video frames, focusing on temporal changes, abstract concepts, and affective states, will generate textual descriptions that are significantly more evocative and useful for poetic inspiration than standard dense captioning or single-image VQA outputs.
proposals.method: We will develop a temporal VQA pipeline. First, keyframes are extracted from input videos. For each frame, a Large Language Model (LLM) like GPT-4V or a specialized VQA model (e.g., based on BLIP-2 or LLaVA) is prompted with a curated chain of questions progressing from concrete (objects, actions) to abstract (mood, metaphor, memory). Drawing from Zhang's work on 'Video Action Differencing', we will design specific modules to contrast Q&A pairs across frames to highlight temporal dynamics. These structured textual outputs will then serve as the context for a fine-tuned poetic language model to generate verse.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel benchmark dataset pairing video clips with structured VQA-derived descriptive text and human-written poetry, advancing evaluation beyond standard VQA tasks; 2) a demonstrated framework for using interpretable, multi-granular VQA as a superior intermediate representation for creative language generation tasks, bridging video understanding and computational creativity.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in repurposing VQA not for answer accuracy, but for controlled, chain-of-thought style descriptive generation tailored for creative writing, and explicitly integrating temporal differencing techniques from action recognition to capture narrative flow essential for poetry.

proposals.title: UniMod: A Universal Modality-Agnostic Transformer for Video Understanding Without Language Priors
proposals.motivation: Current state-of-the-art video-language models (e.g., VideoCLIP, Flamingo) rely on language-specific components like tokenizers, embeddings, and pre-trained LLM heads, creating a linguistic bottleneck. This limits their applicability to non-linguistic reasoning tasks and data-scarce modalities, contradicting the goal of a truly generalist AI. A modality-agnostic architecture could unify processing and improve efficiency and generalization.
proposals.hypothesis: A single-stream transformer, trained from scratch on a unified token sequence of raw video patches and discrete, language-agnostic semantic codes, can achieve competitive performance on established video understanding benchmarks without any explicit linguistic modeling components.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Masked Autoencoder (MAE) framework to video, using ViViT to encode spatiotemporal patches. Inspired by VQ-VAE, we will quantize a subset of these patches into a shared, learnable codebook of 'universal semantic tokens'. A transformer encoder will process a concatenated sequence of continuous video tokens and discrete semantic tokens. For evaluation, we will train task-specific lightweight heads on frozen representations for action recognition (Kinetics), temporal reasoning (Video Action Differencing), and retrieval (YouCook2), deliberately avoiding language-conditioned training.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence for the feasibility of language-agnostic video representation learning. It will deliver a novel model architecture and training protocol that decouples high-level video understanding from linguistic supervision, advancing towards more general and data-efficient multimodal AI.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that injects language as a supervisory signal or aligns modalities post-hoc, this proposal eliminates language-specific processing entirely from the core architecture. It uniquely combines video MAE with a shared, learnable codebook to create a unified token space for heterogeneous signal types.

proposals.title: Beyond Monolingual Specialization: A Video-Language Benchmark for Evaluating Cross-Lingual Transfer and Emergent Multilingual Understanding
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm in video-language AI has been to train and benchmark models on high-resource languages like English, creating a 'single-model-per-language' ecosystem. This is inefficient and fails to leverage potential synergies between languages, limiting accessibility and performance for the vast majority of the world's languages. A systematic investigation into whether a single, massively multilingual video-language model can outperform a collection of specialized monolingual models is critically needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single, properly designed massively multilingual video-language model, trained with a temporally-aware objective, will outperform a suite of individually trained monolingual models on a diverse set of video understanding tasks, due to positive cross-lingual transfer and the emergence of a more robust, language-agnostic visual representation.
proposals.method: We will construct a new multilingual video-language benchmark derived from open-source scientific and instructional video datasets, extending Video Action Differencing and CLIP-style tasks with temporally-grounded captions in 50+ languages. We will train a unified model using a Transformer-based architecture with a shared visual encoder (e.g., ViT) and a multilingual text encoder (e.g., XLM-RoBERTa). The core innovation is the integration of a 'temporal stochastic injection' module during training, which perturbs frame sequences and language alignments to force the model to learn robust, language-invariant spatio-temporal representations, rather than relying on lexical co-occurrences.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a rigorous, large-scale benchmark for evaluating multilingual video-language understanding, 2) empirical evidence for or against the single massive multilingual model paradigm in the video domain, and 3) a novel training methodology using stochastic temporal injection to improve cross-lingual generalization.
proposals.novelty: While multilingual LLMs are common, applying this paradigm to the complex, temporally-grounded video-language domain is unexplored. Our approach uniquely combines insights from stochastic generative modeling for robustness with temporal action differencing to create a stress test for true multilingual understanding beyond simple retrieval.

proposals.title: Temporal-CLIP: Cross-Lingual Video-Language Alignment via Stochastic Temporal Injection and Low-Resource Language Bootstrapping
proposals.motivation: Current large vision-language models (VLMs) like CLIP exhibit significant performance disparities across languages, excelling in high-resource languages (e.g., English) while underperforming for low-resource ones.
