This limits their global applicability and fairness, particularly for video understanding tasks where temporal dynamics add complexity. Addressing this gap is crucial for developing truly generalist AI systems that can serve diverse linguistic communities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that injecting controlled stochastic noise into the temporal alignment process of video-language models, combined with a novel data-efficient bootstrapping strategy using multilingual scientific captions, will significantly improve cross-lingual generalization, especially for low-resource languages.
proposals.method: We will extend the Video Action Differencing framework and integrate principles from stochastic injection for generative modeling. First, we will curate a multilingual video-text benchmark derived from open scientific literature (extending Zhang's prior dataset work), focusing on languages with scarce video data. The core model will be a temporal video encoder aligned with a multilingual LLM. We will introduce a novel training regime: during contrastive learning, we will stochastically inject noise into the temporal attention weights of the video encoder and the token embeddings of low-resource language captions, simulating distribution shifts and forcing robust feature learning. A bootstrapping loop will use the model's own improved predictions on unlabeled multilingual scientific text to iteratively refine alignment.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) A new benchmark and method for evaluating low-resource language performance in video-language tasks. 2) A novel training technique combining stochastic temporal injection with data bootstrapping to improve cross-lingual robustness. 3) Empirical evidence and a released model demonstrating significantly reduced performance disparity across languages in video retrieval and captioning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific application of stochastic injection mechanisms to the temporal dimension of video-language alignment for cross-lingual transfer, a direction not explored in pre-2026 CLIP or video-language literature. Furthermore, leveraging structured scientific captions as a bootstrap anchor for low-resource languages is a new data strategy.

proposals.title: Multilingual Video-Language Temporal Alignment: A Benchmark and Curriculum Learning Framework for Robust Cross-Lingual Generalization
proposals.motivation: Current large-scale video-language models (VLMs) are predominantly trained on English-centric datasets, leading to significant performance degradation on non-English video-text tasks. This limits their global applicability and fairness. Expanding training corpora beyond the standard 112 datasets to include diverse multilingual sources is crucial for building robust, general-purpose VLMs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a curriculum learning strategy that progressively incorporates diverse, multilingual video-text pairs, coupled with a novel temporal grounding loss, will significantly improve a model's cross-lingual robustness and temporal reasoning capabilities compared to simple data concatenation.
proposals.method: We will extend the CLIP/ViT-L architecture with a temporal transformer to process video clips. We will curate a new multilingual benchmark, 'VideoLang-Temp', comprising video clips with parallel captions in 10+ languages and fine-grained temporal annotations. Training will employ a two-stage curriculum: first on high-resource language pairs for alignment, then on lower-resource pairs with stochastic injection of noisy translations to simulate real-world distribution shifts. The loss function will combine standard contrastive loss with the 'Video Action Differencing' loss to enforce precise temporal grounding across languages.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel, publicly available multilingual video-language benchmark focused on temporal reasoning, 2) a robust, open-source VLM with demonstrably improved cross-lingual performance, and 3) a validated curriculum learning framework for multilingual multimodal training.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of curriculum-based multilingual training with explicit temporal grounding objectives for VLMs, moving beyond static image-text alignment to address the spatiotemporal and linguistic complexity of video understanding.

proposals.title: VidAug-NLP: Cross-Modal Data Augmentation for Low-Resource Language Tasks via Vision-Inspired Stochastic Injection
proposals.motivation: Low-resource languages suffer from severe data scarcity, hindering the development of equitable NLP models. While data augmentation is a cornerstone of robust computer vision, its direct application to NLP, especially for structured text generation and understanding tasks, remains underexplored. This work addresses the critical need for scalable, high-quality synthetic data generation methods tailored to the linguistic nuances of low-resource settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that principled, vision-inspired data augmentation techniques—specifically stochastic injection methods that perturb latent representations—can be effectively adapted to NLP to generate diverse and realistic synthetic text data, thereby significantly improving model performance on low-resource language tasks.
proposals.method: We will adapt the 'Stochastic Injection' framework from Zhang's prior generative modeling work to the text domain. Using a pre-trained multilingual language model (e.g., mT5 or XLM-R) as a feature extractor, we will inject controlled noise into the latent representations of authentic low-resource language samples during fine-tuning. This process, analogous to visual data augmentation like CutMix or noise injection in VAEs, will generate augmented textual representations. A decoder will then reconstruct these perturbed latents into fluent, semantically consistent synthetic training examples for downstream tasks like named entity recognition or question answering.
proposals.contribution: This project will deliver: 1) A novel, cross-modal augmentation pipeline (VidAug-NLP) that translates vision-based stochastic methods to NLP, 2) New benchmark datasets for low-resource languages augmented with high-quality synthetic data, and 3) Empirical evidence on the efficacy of representation-level augmentation for structured NLP tasks, advancing the field of data-centric AI for linguistic diversity.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic cross-modal transfer of advanced vision augmentation techniques (stochastic injection) to NLP's latent space, moving beyond simple token-level substitutions, and in targeting the generation of coherent data for complex, structured language tasks in low-resource contexts.

proposals.title: MetaCLIP-Lang: Meta-Learning for Few-Shot Cross-Lingual Adaptation of Vision-Language Models
proposals.motivation: Large vision-language models (VLMs) like CLIP are predominantly English-centric, limiting their global applicability. Adapting these models to new languages typically requires extensive, language-specific data and compute, which is infeasible for low-resource languages. A method for rapid, data-efficient language adaptation is crucial for democratizing multimodal AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-learning framework, which trains a VLM's text encoder to rapidly adapt its embedding space from minimal language-specific examples, will enable effective few-shot cross-lingual transfer, outperforming standard fine-tuning in low-data regimes.
proposals.method: We will extend the MAML (Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning) algorithm to the VLM domain. The 'inner loop' will simulate few-shot adaptation tasks, where the model learns to adjust its text encoder's projection layers using small support sets from various languages (e.g., Spanish, Swahili, Bengali). The 'outer loop' will update the model's meta-parameters to optimize for post-adaptation performance on query sets. We will benchmark on a derived multi-language version of the Video Action Differencing task and a new cross-lingual image-text retrieval benchmark built from open scientific literature data.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel meta-learning framework for efficient cross-lingual VLM adaptation, 2) a systematic benchmark for evaluating few-shot language adaptation in VLMs, and 3) empirical evidence on the data efficiency and performance limits of such methods compared to standard fine-tuning.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been explored for vision tasks and LLM adaptation, its application to the specific problem of few-shot language adaptation for frozen, pre-trained vision-language models is novel. It uniquely combines Zhang's expertise in video-language benchmarks and stochastic injection methods (for robust inner-loop updates) with meta-learning principles.

proposals.title: Temporally-Aware Masked Modeling for Video-Language Pre-training
proposals.motivation: Current video-language models often rely on contrastive pre-training (e.g., CLIP-style), which excels at global alignment but may underperform on tasks requiring fine-grained, temporal understanding of events and object state changes. This gap is critical for benchmarks like Video Action Differencing, where the model must reason about temporal evolution. A BERT-like masked modeling objective, proven for language and adapted for images, offers a promising but underexplored path for learning dense, temporally-sensitive video-language representations.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a temporally-structured masked modeling objective into the initial pre-training layers of a video-language pipeline will significantly improve performance on downstream tasks requiring fine-grained temporal and causal reasoning, compared to models trained solely with contrastive objectives.
proposals.method: We will extend the Video Action Differencing framework by adding a dedicated pre-training stage. The model will be a dual-encoder architecture (video transformer, text transformer) with early fusion layers. We will design a novel masking strategy: for video, we will mask spatio-temporal patches and object tracklets; for text, we will mask action verbs and temporal connectors. The model will be trained to reconstruct the masked content using a joint cross-modal encoder, employing a loss combining masked language modeling (MLM) and masked video modeling (MVM) objectives. This self-supervised task will be followed by standard contrastive alignment training.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new pre-training recipe for video-language models that enhances temporal reasoning. It will yield a strong baseline model and a set of diagnostic benchmarks for evaluating fine-grained video-language understanding. The findings will inform the design of more data-efficient and semantically grounded biomedical or general-purpose vision-language generalists.
proposals.novelty: While masked modeling is common in language and vision, its application to *joint* video-language pre-training with a focus on temporal structure—specifically masking object tracks and action words—is novel. This moves beyond frame-level or token-level reconstruction to model the dynamics of events described in text.

proposals.title: DocCLIP: A Hierarchical Transformer for Document-Level Video-Language Alignment
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models like CLIP operate on short video clips and sentence-level captions, failing to capture the complex, long-range narrative structure of full documents (e.g., instructional videos with chapters, scientific lectures). This limits their utility for real-world tasks requiring holistic understanding, such as dense video captioning or long-form video QA. Scaling to document-level processing is a critical next step for robust multimodal AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical transformer architecture, which first models intra-segment dependencies and then aggregates information across segments with a temporal attention mechanism, will significantly outperform sentence-level models on tasks requiring document-level video-language reasoning.
proposals.method: We will extend the CLIP framework by processing a video document as a sequence of semantically coherent segments (using pre-2026 scene detection or ASR timestamps). A base Vision Transformer (ViT) and text encoder will extract features per segment. A novel Temporal Coherence Transformer (TCT) layer will then model cross-segment dependencies in both modalities. We will train the model using a contrastive loss at the segment level and a novel document-level matching loss, leveraging large-scale datasets derived from structured sources like scientific literature videos (inspired by Zhang's prior work).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a new model architecture for document-level video-language alignment, 2) a benchmark for evaluating long-range multimodal dependencies, extending existing video-language benchmarks, and 3) empirical evidence on the importance of hierarchical modeling for complex video understanding.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of a temporal transformer for cross-segment reasoning within the contrastive learning paradigm of CLIP, moving beyond simple pooling of clip-level features. This addresses a key gap in modeling narrative or procedural flow across long videos.

proposals.title: VideoCLIP-Ochestra: A Federated Modular Architecture for Efficient Video-Language Understanding
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm in AI is scaling massive, monolithic models (e.g., large vision-language models), which is computationally prohibitive and lacks the functional specialization seen in biological cognition. A modular, distributed approach could achieve similar capabilities with greater efficiency and interpretability, directly addressing the computational and environmental costs of current large-scale AI. This is especially pertinent for complex, temporally extended modalities like video, where monolithic models struggle with efficient spatio-temporal reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: A federated system of smaller, pre-trained expert modules (for visual encoding, temporal dynamics, and language understanding), dynamically orchestrated by a lightweight network, can match or exceed the performance of a monolithic large model on video-language benchmarks while activating fewer parameters per task.
proposals.method: We will construct a system with three fixed expert modules: 1) a CLIP-based visual encoder, 2) a temporal reasoning module inspired by Video Action Differencing for dynamic feature extraction, and 3) a language specialist (a frozen LLM). A trainable 'Orchestration Network' (a small transformer or Mixture-of-Experts router) will process the input query to generate soft routing weights. It will learn to dynamically combine features from the experts via weighted aggregation and cross-attention, synthesizing a final output. Training will focus solely on the Orchestration Network's parameters using a contrastive loss on datasets like HowTo100M.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, scalable blueprint for modular AI systems in video-language tasks, demonstrating a path beyond monolithic scaling. It will deliver a new benchmark for evaluating parameter efficiency in multimodal models and offer insights into the functional decomposition of complex AI tasks.
proposals.novelty: While modular AI and mixture-of-experts exist, this proposal uniquely grounds its architecture in the theory of Distributed Cognition and applies it to the under-explored, high-cost domain of video understanding. The novelty lies in the explicit functional segregation (temporal vs. visual vs. linguistic) and the use of a lightweight, input-dependent router to synthesize outputs from frozen, pre-existing models.

proposals.title: Adversarial Reality-Gap Closure: A Self-Improving Training Loop for Robust Video-Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current large-scale video-language models are trained on massive, often noisy, web-curated datasets, leading to brittle generalization and poor performance on real-world, long-tail distributions. Conversely, training solely on small, high-quality curated datasets fails to capture the complexity of real data, creating a significant reality gap. This work addresses the core challenge of maintaining high-quality training standards while systematically expanding model robustness to real-world complexity.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid training paradigm, where an adversarial network actively probes a core model's blind spots on broad data and converts failures into high-quality training examples, will create a self-improving loop that significantly closes the reality gap without sacrificing the integrity of the curated seed data.
proposals.method: We propose a two-network system: a core Video-Language Transformer (e.g., a CLIP-style model) trained on a verified seed corpus, and a separate adversarial Temporal Transformer network. The adversarial network, trained on a broad, uncurated dataset, generates challenging video-text pairs (e.g., via temporal perturbation or caption corruption inspired by 'Video Action Differencing') to probe the core model. Failure cases are automatically filtered and refined using a rule-based or a small LLM-based (e.g., GPT-3.5) verifier to ensure quality and relevance, then added to the core training set in a principled, curriculum-based manner.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel training framework for robust video-language alignment that systematically mitigates distributional shift. We will provide a new benchmark for evaluating robustness to real-world distribution shifts in video-language tasks and demonstrate a model with superior generalization compared to models trained solely on curated or web-scale data.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard adversarial training which perturbs inputs, this method uses an adversarial network to discover and *construct* new, high-quality training data targeting specific weaknesses. It merges the principles of curated data integrity with the coverage of web-scale data through an automated, closed-loop system.

proposals.title: Temporal Adapters: Parameter-Efficient Specialization of Vision-Language Models for Video Understanding
proposals.motivation: Large pre-trained vision-language models (VLMs) like CLIP are foundational for many tasks, but full fine-tuning for video understanding is computationally prohibitive and risks catastrophic forgetting. Existing parameter-efficient methods (e.g., LoRA, prompt tuning) are primarily designed for language models and do not explicitly address the temporal reasoning and action dynamics central to video tasks. A method is needed to efficiently adapt VLMs to video while preserving their robust visual-linguistic alignment and enabling multi-task specialization.
proposals.hypothesis: Inserting lightweight, trainable adapter modules with explicit temporal modeling capabilities into a frozen VLM backbone will enable efficient and effective adaptation to diverse video understanding tasks, outperforming full fine-tuning and existing adapters in parameter efficiency and mitigating forgetting.
proposals.method: We will design a novel Temporal Adapter module, inspired by the plugin analogy, to be inserted after the transformer layers in a frozen CLIP-like model. The adapter will integrate a small, learnable temporal difference network (informed by Video Action Differencing) to model frame-to-frame changes, followed by a bottleneck feed-forward layer. During adaptation for tasks like action recognition or video QA, only the adapter parameters are optimized. We will benchmark this against full fine-tuning and methods like LoRA on established video datasets, measuring performance, training cost, and the model's ability to retain original capabilities.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new parameter-efficient transfer learning method specifically tailored for temporal data, enabling a single VLM to host multiple video task specialists. It will advance the field by providing a concrete, efficient pathway to create biomedical or generalist video AI systems from large-scale pre-trained models, as motivated by recent dataset efforts.
proposals.novelty: While adapters exist, our proposal uniquely combines the plugin-based specialization concept with explicit, lightweight temporal modeling mechanisms for video, a synergy not explored in pre-2026 VLM adaptation literature. It directly applies Zhang's expertise in video, language, and differencing to a core problem in efficient transfer learning.

proposals.title: SAEA-Video: Semantic Gap Characterization and Architecture Co-Design for Efficient Edge Video Understanding
proposals.motivation: Deploying vision-language models on edge devices for real-time video understanding is hindered by naive scaling of large architectures, leading to inefficiency and poor performance on domain-specific edge data (e.g., low-frame-rate, non-naturalistic sensor streams). Current methods focus on pruning or distilling large models, ignoring the fundamental semantic and statistical divergence between web-scale training data and constrained edge modalities. A principled framework to first quantify this modality gap and then architect models to bridge it is critically needed for performant, efficient edge AI.
proposals.hypothesis: The performance of small models on edge video tasks is limited primarily by a quantifiable semantic mismatch to their data domain, and co-designing model inductive biases and training based on this characterization will yield superior efficiency and accuracy compared to scaled-down general-purpose architectures.
proposals.method: We will first develop a formal metric, building on concepts from domain adaptation and Zhang's prior work on 'Video Action Differencing', to quantify the semantic and temporal distribution shift of a target edge video stream (e.g., drone footage, wearable camera feed) from standard web video datasets. Second, we will co-design a lightweight, spatio-temporal architecture where core components (e.g., attention mechanisms, convolution kernels) are explicitly parameterized and initialized to align with the identified gap profile. Third, training will employ a tailored objective combining reconstruction, a novel semantic consistency loss derived from our gap metric, and efficient distillation from a large vision-language model (VLMs/LLMs as in Zhang's recent work) acting as a semantic anchor.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel, actionable framework for designing edge models grounded in semantic gap analysis, not just compression. 2) A new benchmark suite for evaluating edge video models under explicit modality divergence. 3) Open-sourced, highly efficient model blueprints for specific edge video domains (e.g., aerial monitoring, egocentric action recognition) that outperform similarly-sized generic models.
proposals.novelty: This work moves beyond model compression by making the semantic modality gap the primary design variable, introducing a 'measure-then-design' paradigm distinct from post-hoc adaptation or scaling. It uniquely integrates formal gap characterization, architecture co-design, and VLM-guided training for edge-specific model synthesis.

proposals.title: Temporal-Consistent Video-Language Coding via Synthetic Action Differencing and Stochastic Injection
proposals.motivation: Current video-language models rely on massive, unlabeled web-scale datasets for pretraining, which are unavailable in specialized domains like biomedical procedures or sensitive environments. This data scarcity creates a significant barrier to developing effective coding models for temporal understanding tasks. The problem is acute for video action recognition and description, where temporal coherence is critical but labeled data is sparse.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a video-language coding model can be effectively trained on a limited corpus of labeled videos by generating temporally consistent synthetic video-language pairs and applying stochastic injection techniques to regularize the learned representation space.
proposals.method: We will leverage the Video Action Differencing technique to generate synthetic video variations and corresponding language descriptions by altering action sequences in a small seed set of labeled videos. A dual-encoder architecture (e.g., CLIP-style) will be trained on this augmented set. To improve robustness and distributional coverage, we will integrate the three forms of stochastic injection (e.g., noise in feature space, latent perturbations) from Zhang's prior work directly into the contrastive learning objective, enforcing temporal consistency across synthetic and real video-language pairs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for training video-language models under severe data constraints, directly applicable to specialized benchmarks. It will advance methods for synthetic temporal data generation and demonstrate how stochastic regularization can compensate for limited pretraining scale. The resulting models will be evaluated on established temporal understanding tasks to validate their efficacy.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic combination of synthetic temporal data generation via action differencing with distribution-to-distribution stochastic injection for regularization, a approach not yet explored for data-efficient video-language coding. This moves beyond standard data augmentation or semi-supervised learning by explicitly modeling and regularizing the temporal structure of the synthetic data distribution.

proposals.title: StanzaBio: Knowledge Distillation from BioBERT for Efficient Biomedical NLP in Low-Resource Settings
proposals.motivation: Large biomedical language models like BioBERT achieve state-of-the-art performance but are computationally prohibitive for widespread deployment in resource-constrained environments like clinical point-of-care or mobile health applications. There is a critical need for efficient, specialized models that retain domain-specific knowledge while being fast and lightweight. Knowledge distillation offers a promising path to bridge this gap, but effective distillation strategies for complex biomedical NLP tasks require careful design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured knowledge distillation framework, transferring both contextual embeddings and task-specific decision boundaries from BioBERT to the Stanza architecture, can produce a model that achieves competitive performance on biomedical NLP benchmarks while being significantly more parameter- and inference-time efficient.
proposals.method: We will design a multi-stage distillation pipeline. First, we will use response-based distillation on the BLURB benchmark suite, training the Stanza model to mimic BioBERT's logits on named entity recognition and relation extraction. Second, we will employ feature-based distillation, aligning intermediate layer representations using a temporal attention mechanism inspired by Video Action Differencing to weight the importance of different token representations. Finally, we will inject controlled stochastic noise during training, a method shown effective in Zhang's prior work, to improve the robustness of the distilled model's generalization.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver 'StanzaBio,' a publicly released, efficient model for biomedical text processing, alongside a reproducible distillation recipe. It will provide empirical analysis on the trade-offs between model size, inference speed, and accuracy on key biomedical tasks, offering practical guidance for deploying NLP in real-world medical and research settings.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting temporal attention mechanisms from video analysis to weight token-level knowledge transfer in distillation and in systematically applying stochastic injection techniques to improve the robustness of the distilled biomedical model, a combination not explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: BiomedCLIP-MT: Multi-Task Learning for Cross-Domain Vision-Language Transfer in Biomedical Video Understanding
proposals.motivation: Current biomedical vision-language models, such as those derived from large-scale scientific literature datasets, often specialize narrowly, limiting their ability to leverage rich temporal and contextual knowledge from general-domain video-language tasks. This creates a barrier for developing robust biomedical generalist AI capable of complex reasoning over clinical or biological video sequences. A unified model that can jointly reason across general and biomedical domains is needed to improve transfer and performance on specialized tasks like video action differencing in medical procedures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single vision-language transformer, trained via multi-task learning on a mixture of general video-language tasks (e.g., temporal grounding, action recognition) and biomedical tasks (e.g., scientific figure captioning, procedure step localization), will demonstrate superior transfer learning and generalization on downstream biomedical video benchmarks compared to models trained on either domain alone.
proposals.method: We will extend the CLIP/ViT architecture with a temporal encoder (e.g., TimeSformer) to process video clips. The model will be trained with a multi-task objective combining losses from: 1) contrastive image/video-text alignment on general (e.g., WebVid) and biomedical (e.g., derived from open scientific literature) datasets, 2) masked language modeling for biomedical text, and 3) temporal action differencing prediction. We will employ techniques like stochastic injection for robust feature fusion across domains and task-conditioned adapters to manage task interference while sharing a core backbone.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a publicly available, multi-task vision-language model for biomedical video analysis, demonstrating improved zero-shot and few-shot transfer on benchmarks like MedVidQA or surgical action segmentation. It will provide a framework for integrating general-domain temporal reasoning with domain-specific knowledge, advancing the development of biomedical generalist AI.
proposals.novelty: While multi-task learning is common in NLP, its application to joint general-biomedical vision-language modeling, specifically for video understanding with temporal constraints, is unexplored. The novel integration of action differencing tasks with standard contrastive pre-training creates a unique inductive bias for temporal reasoning transfer.

proposals.title: HELM-T: A Temporal Benchmarking Framework for Tracking the Evolution of Language and Vision-Language Models
proposals.motivation: The rapid, continuous release of new and updated language models (LMs) and vision-language models (VLMs) creates a moving target for evaluation. Pre-2026 benchmarks like HELM provide crucial snapshots, but lack a structured methodology for tracking performance evolution over time, especially on temporally-sensitive tasks. This gap hinders our understanding of whether model improvements are consistent across domains and how capabilities shift with new training data and architectural variants.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematic temporal benchmarking will reveal that model performance improvements are non-uniform across task types and that newer model versions exhibit significant drift on tasks requiring knowledge of temporally-grounded information, even as aggregate scores increase.
proposals.method: We will extend the HELM framework to create HELM-T, introducing a continuous evaluation pipeline. This involves: 1) Curating a dynamic benchmark suite that includes static core scenarios (e.g., MMLU) and a new 'temporal differencing' task for video/text, inspired by Zhang's prior work, to test knowledge recency and reasoning over changes. 2) Automating the regular evaluation of a curated set of model families (e.g., Llama, GPT, Claude, Flamingo, GPT-4V) and their successive versions via standardized API calls. 3) Employing time-series analysis and statistical significance testing to quantify performance trajectories and identify regressions or breakthroughs specific to modalities (language, vision-language) and task categories.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with the first longitudinal dataset of model capability evolution, a set of methodologies for temporal evaluation, and actionable insights into the stability and specialization of model improvements. It will establish a new standard for reporting model progress beyond static leaderboards.
proposals.novelty: While HELM benchmarks statically, this proposal introduces a *temporal dimension* to holistic evaluation, explicitly tracking version-by-version changes. It uniquely integrates temporal reasoning tasks from video/action differencing into a broad LM/VLM benchmarking framework to probe a model's dynamic world knowledge.

proposals.title: MM-Holistic: A Unified Benchmark and Diagnostic Framework for Holistic Evaluation of Video-Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current evaluation of video-language models is fragmented, relying on disparate, narrow benchmarks (e.g., action recognition, text-to-video retrieval) that fail to capture holistic understanding. This makes it difficult to diagnose model weaknesses, compare progress, and guide development towards robust, general-purpose multimodal AI. A standardized, comprehensive evaluation suite is urgently needed, especially for temporal reasoning and fine-grained video-text alignment, which are critical for real-world applications.
proposals.hypothesis: A unified, multi-dimensional evaluation framework that systematically probes video-language models across complementary axes (e.g., temporal dynamics, compositional reasoning, action differencing) will reveal significant, previously obscured performance gaps and provide more actionable diagnostic insights than current task-specific benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will construct MM-Holistic, a benchmark integrating three core components: 1) A curated dataset synthesizing existing tasks (e.g., from ActivityNet, HowTo100M) with novel, procedurally generated video-language examples emphasizing temporal dependencies and fine-grained attributes. 2) A standardized evaluation protocol using LLMs (e.g., GPT-4 as a judge) for automated, flexible scoring of open-ended generation tasks, alongside traditional metrics. 3) A diagnostic dashboard that visualizes model performance across distinct capability axes, inspired by Zhang's prior work on 'Video Action Differencing' and stochastic injection for robust modeling.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a standardized, open-source benchmark and toolkit for holistic video-language model evaluation, enabling more rigorous comparisons. It will produce a detailed diagnostic analysis of state-of-the-art models (e.g., VideoCLIP, Flamingo), identifying specific failure modes in temporal and compositional reasoning to guide future research.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing benchmarks, MM-Holistic unifies disparate evaluation dimensions into a single diagnostic framework, explicitly integrating temporal reasoning diagnostics (inspired by action differencing) and leveraging LLM-based judges for scalable, nuanced assessment of generative video-language tasks.

proposals.title: TemporalBERT: Advancing Video-Language Benchmarking with Pre-trained Transformer Encoders for Contextual Note Modeling
proposals.motivation: Video-language benchmarks, such as those derived from open scientific literature, often rely on textual notes (e.g., descriptions, metadata) to ground video understanding tasks. Pre-2026, many video-language models use LSTMs to encode these notes, which struggle with long-range dependencies and lack the rich contextual priors of modern large-scale language models. This limits the model's ability to leverage the full semantic and temporal nuance present in descriptive text, hindering performance on complex tasks like action differencing or biomedical AI generalization.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing LSTM-based note encoders with pre-trained transformer models (e.g., BERT, RoBERTa) will provide superior contextual representations, leading to significant performance gains on video-language understanding benchmarks that require fine-grained temporal and semantic alignment.
proposals.method: We will integrate a frozen or fine-tuned BERT-base model as the text encoder within established video-language architectures (e.g., CLIP-style or task-specific models). For temporal tasks, we will explore methods to inject temporal awareness, such as using the transformer's output embeddings as inputs to a temporal reasoning module or employing attention mechanisms between text tokens and learned video clip features. The model will be evaluated on relevant pre-2026 benchmarks, including action differencing datasets and the proposed large-scale biomedical video-language dataset, using standard metrics for retrieval, classification, and temporal localization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous empirical analysis of pre-trained transformers for contextual note encoding in video tasks, establishing a new strong baseline. It will yield a publicly available model, TemporalBERT, that demonstrates improved generalization and temporal reasoning, directly advancing the development of biomedical and general-purpose video-language AI.
proposals.novelty: While transformers are common in NLP, their systematic integration and evaluation for encoding descriptive notes in video-language benchmarks—particularly for temporal tasks like action differencing—is not yet a settled practice. The novelty lies in the specific architectural fusion and targeted evaluation on temporal video-language understanding, moving beyond simple video-text retrieval.

ideas.title: Semantic-Aware Edge Architectures (SAEA): Designing for modality gaps, not just parameters
ideas.description: This research direction posits that the primary barrier to effective small models on edge devices is not optimization or parameter count, but the semantic mismatch between a general-purpose model architecture and the specific data modality it must process locally (e.g., sensor streams, low-power vision). We will develop a design framework for creating highly efficient, specialized edge models by first formally characterizing the fundamental semantic divergence of a target edge modality from standard web data, and then co-designing the model's inductive biases, architecture, and training objectives to directly bridge this identified gap. This results in edge models that are not just scaled-down versions of large models, but are fundamentally architected for semantic alignment with their constrained, real-world data domain from inception.

ideas.title: Adapter Modules for Model Specialization via Parameter-Efficient Transfer
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that adapting a pre-trained model to a downstream task requires fine-tuning all its parameters. Drawing an analogy from software engineering's plugin architecture—where core application integrity is preserved by adding external, modular functionality through APIs—we propose a method for parameter-efficient transfer learning. The analogous solution involves creating lightweight plugins instead of modifying core source code. Adapted to machine learning, this translates to designing small, trainable 'adapter' modules that are inserted between the frozen layers of a base model (e.g., a large language model or vision transformer). During adaptation for a specific task, only the parameters of these adapter modules are optimized, preserving the integrity, knowledge, and stability of the original pre-trained model. This approach reduces computational cost, prevents catastrophic forgetting, and allows a single base model to host multiple specialized adapters simultaneously.

ideas.title: Meta-Learned Initialization for Adaptive Modality Gap Control
ideas.description: This research proposes a meta-optimization framework that learns a network initialization policy, rather than prescribing a fixed gap. A meta-learner, trained across a distribution of related multimodal tasks, optimizes the initial parameters of a base model such that after a short period of standard fine-tuning on a new task, the resulting model exhibits a robust and beneficial modality gap. This approach directly addresses the brittleness of a hand-designed gap by adapting the initial conditions to the data and architecture. The outcome is a learned initialization scheme that provides a stronger, more generalizable starting point for training aligned multimodal models.

ideas.title: Federated Domain Adaptation for Modular Multimodal Alignment
ideas.description: This research direction investigates how principles from unsupervised domain adaptation can be applied within a federated or modular architecture to align different data modalities (e.g., vision, language, audio). Instead of a single massive model, the system would consist of smaller, specialized modules that learn to project their features into a shared, task-aligned latent space by adapting to each other's distributions in a decentralized manner. This approach challenges the necessity for monolithic scale by distributing the alignment objective, allowing capabilities to emerge from the collaborative adaptation of components. It inherently supports privacy-preserving learning on distributed data and enables flexible, scalable composition of multimodal functionalities.

ideas.title: Causal Intervention Framework for Diagnosing Representational Bottlenecks in Multi-Modal Models
ideas.description: This research proposes a causal framework to diagnose the structure and impact of modality gaps in models with three or more modalities. Instead of merely measuring pairwise distances, the approach uses systematic interventions—such as ablating or noise-perturbing specific modality encoders during training or inference—to construct a directed graph of representational influence. This graph reveals which modalities act as primary bottlenecks or dominant anchors in the shared representation space and which gaps are most detrimental to downstream task performance. The outcome is a principled methodology for guiding architectural choices, loss function design, and training data curation to mitigate the most harmful multi-modal discrepancies.

ideas.title: Modular Intelligence via Distributed Cognitive Architectures
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that scaling massive, monolithic models is necessary for advanced AI capabilities by drawing an analogy from cognitive neuroscience. The analogous field is Distributed Cognition, which explains how the human brain achieves general intelligence through a federated network of specialized, modular regions (visual cortex, language centers, memory systems) that communicate, rather than through a single homogeneous processing unit. The analogous solution is this modular, functionally segregated architecture where capabilities emerge from interaction. Adapted to AI research, the idea is to design a federated system of smaller, pre-trained expert modules, each optimized for a distinct cognitive function (e.g., syntax parsing, visual reasoning, factual memory). A lightweight, trainable 'orchestration network' would learn to dynamically route inputs and synthesize outputs from this ensemble. The goal is to achieve similar capabilities to monolithic large models by leveraging modular specialization and efficient resource allocation, potentially reducing total computational cost and active parameter count per task.

ideas.title: Semantic Divergence as a Data Geometry Problem: An Analogy from Geophysics
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that the performance gap between modalities in multimodal models is primarily due to optimization difficulties. By drawing an analogy from geophysics—where the reconstruction of subsurface structures is limited first by sensor placement and data coverage, not just inversion algorithms—we adapt the methodology of resolution testing. The adapted research idea is to treat different modalities as imperfect 'sensors' of a shared semantic reality. The core experiment involves creating a synthetic or tightly controlled environment where the 'true' aligned semantic space is known. By systematically degrading or altering the geometric coverage of each modality's data in this space while using a fixed, near-optimal model architecture and training regimen, we can quantify the performance gap that persists. This residual gap is attributed not to failed optimization, but to the fundamental semantic divergence and incomplete overlap in the data manifolds of the modalities—the 'inverse problem' is ill-posed by data nature, not solvers. The solution is to develop diagnostic tests, analogous to geophysical checkerboard tests, to isolate and measure this data-induced causal gap.

ideas.title: Adversarial Reality-Gap Closure for Robust High-Quality Training
ideas.description: This research investigates a hybrid training paradigm designed to overcome the generalization failure of purely curated datasets. A core model is initially trained on a verified, high-quality seed corpus, while a separate adversarial network, exposed to broad data, actively probes for blind spots and distributional shifts. The system automatically converts these failure cases into new, principled training examples that are both high-quality and targeted at specific weaknesses. This creates a self-improving loop where curated data quality is maintained, but the model's robustness is systematically expanded to cover the true complexity of real-world domains.

ideas.title: Causal Disentanglement of the Modality Gap via Isomorphic Data and Optimal Protocol Matching
ideas.description: This research rigorously tests the causal contribution of data structure versus optimization to cross-modal performance gaps. It constructs a synthetic data ecosystem where semantic content is perfectly aligned across modalities but expressed through their native statistical structures (e.g., images, text, audio of the same concepts). A single, powerful, and modality-agnostic optimization protocol (like a Perceiver or a unified transformer) is applied under identical constraints to learn from each isomorphic data stream. The residual performance differences after this optimal protocol alignment are then attributed to fundamental data-structural limits, providing a quantifiable, causal measure of the 'semantic divergence' gap.

ideas.title: Apply Optimal Transport with Regularization for Robust Cross-Modal Alignment
ideas.description: This idea expands the original application of optimal transport (OT) to bridge the modality gap by drawing an analogy from computational hydrology. In river engineering, OT regularized with entropy or physical constraints is used to find robust mappings between upstream and downstream sediment distributions despite sparse, noisy data. Adapting this solution, we propose to align modality distributions (e.g., visual and linguistic representations) not with a standard OT formulation, but with a regularized one. Incorporating an entropic or Lipschitz regularization term into the OT objective will yield a smoother, more generalizable transport plan. This approach enhances robustness against the inherent noise and high-dimensional sparsity of neural embeddings, providing a more reliable and stable method for cross-modal alignment in multimodal machine learning systems.

ideas.title: Orchestrated Modular Federation for Emergent Capability Retention
ideas.description: This research designs and tests a dynamically orchestrated federation of smaller, specialized models to replicate the emergent reasoning abilities of giant monolithic models. The core innovation is a trainable orchestrator that learns to decompose complex queries and synthesize module outputs, with the entire system undergoing large-scale co-training on tasks specifically designed to probe emergent capabilities. The goal is to demonstrate that the federation can achieve performance parity on benchmarks requiring high-level reasoning, while maintaining the efficiency, updatability, and privacy benefits of a modular architecture. Success would provide a concrete architectural blueprint for bypassing the need for massive, singular models.

ideas.title: Synthetic Data Curation via Pharmaceutical High-Throughput Screening Analogy
ideas.description: This idea inverts the assumption of broad, general-purpose training data by drawing an analogy from pharmaceutical drug discovery. In that field, the analogous problem of finding effective drugs shifted from testing broad collections of random natural products to using high-throughput screening of purpose-built, diverse synthetic compound libraries designed to probe specific biological targets. The analogous solution is the creation of these optimized synthetic libraries. Adapting this solution, the research would investigate training AI models on algorithmically curated or generated synthetic data libraries. These libraries would be engineered not for volume or web-likeness, but to densely and systematically cover a targeted, high-value latent conceptual space (e.g., chains of reasoning, ethical dilemmas, verifiable facts). The training objective transforms into a directed, efficient search through this engineered data space to find model parameters that optimally activate desired capabilities, moving from passive ingestion of internet-scale data to active, hypothesis-driven synthesis of training environments.

ideas.title: Optimal Transport-Guided Initialization for Controlled Multimodal Alignment
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel initialization scheme for multimodal neural networks where the initial parameters of the modality-specific encoders are not set randomly but are instead derived from an optimal transport plan computed between samples of the pre-training data distributions. This ensures the initial representations in the joint embedding space are already geometrically aligned according to a principled statistical distance, effectively presetting a 'beneficial gap'. Consequently, the model begins training from a state of reduced distributional discrepancy, which accelerates convergence, improves the stability of subsequent fine-tuning alignment, and provides a strong theoretical prior that mitigates the risk of poor local minima.

ideas.title: Structured Multi-Subspace Adversarial Alignment with Information Preservation
ideas.description: This approach reframes modality alignment by decomposing representations into shared and private subspaces, applying adversarial domain adaptation solely to the shared component to achieve alignment without forcing invariance on all features. It employs auxiliary reconstruction and downstream task losses to preserve modality-specific information necessary for performance. Furthermore, it integrates mutual information constraints to prevent the shared subspace from becoming degenerate, ensuring robust and informative aligned representations. This results in a more flexible and powerful framework that explicitly balances alignment with the preservation of critical modality-unique characteristics.

ideas.title: Task-Preserving Optimal Transport with Discriminative Regularization for Cross-Modal Alignment
ideas.description: This redesigned system addresses the structural collapse problem by formulating cross-modal alignment as a regularized optimal transport problem with three key components: a Wasserstein distance term for distribution matching, a contrastive regularization term that preserves within-modality discriminative structure, and a task-specific loss that ensures aligned representations remain useful for downstream objectives. The framework employs adaptive transport plans that prioritize alignment of semantically similar regions while maintaining separation of distinct categories within each modality. This prevents the loss of discriminative features that occurs when forcing rigid distribution matching, ensuring the aligned representations retain both cross-modal correspondence and within-modality discriminative power for practical applications.

ideas.title: Architecting Data-Centric Ecosystems for Causal Specialization
ideas.description: This research inverts the standard assumption that optimization pressure is the primary driver of model convergence. Instead, it posits that the fundamental, causal gap is the irreducible semantic divergence between data modalities (e.g., text, vision, robotics). It then designs technical and economic ecosystems that treat these data gaps as valuable niches. By creating protocols for data provenance, cross-modal value attribution, and composable interfaces, the ecosystem incentivizes the development of specialized foundation models that deeply exploit specific data semantics, making homogenization a suboptimal outcome rather than an inevitability.

ideas.title: An Empirically-Grounded Theory of Modality Gap Effects on Multimodal Fairness
ideas.description: This research develops a formal theoretical framework to explain how the modality gap influences model fairness, but crucially grounds it in a cycle of empirical validation and refinement. The theory generates specific, testable predictions about fairness outcomes across different gap geometries and data distributions. These predictions are then tested on controlled synthetic datasets and real-world multimodal benchmarks, using causal analysis to attribute disparities to the gap mechanism. The theory is iteratively revised based on empirical results, ensuring it produces actionable insights for designing fairer multimodal architectures and training protocols.

ideas.title: Crystallization Seeding for Modality Gap Control
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from materials science, where controlled seeding dictates crystal growth, this idea proposes novel parameter initialization schemes to preset a beneficial initial modality gap. In crystallography, a seed crystal with a specific structure is introduced to control nucleation and ensure desired monocrystalline growth from a supersaturated solution. Adapted to multimodal machine learning, the solution is to design deterministic 'modality seed' initializations for early fusion or cross-modal attention layers. These preset weight patterns are engineered to create a structured, non-random initial representational gap between input modalities (e.g., text and image). This controlled starting condition acts as a guide, steering the subsequent gradient descent optimization to develop a more organized and effective joint embedding space efficiently, analogous to how a seed crystal directs the growth of a larger, perfectly structured crystal from the outset.

ideas.title: Causal Proxies: Interpretable Models to Diagnose Semantic Divergence as the Primary Failure Source
ideas.description: This research direction constructs small, interpretable proxy models that are deliberately architectured to have different inherent assumptions about the semantic alignment (or divergence) between modalities like text, image, and audio. By training these proxies to replicate specific failure patterns observed in large foundation models, we can perform controlled ablation studies to see which semantic alignment assumptions most directly cause or mitigate the failures. This inverts the typical causality question, moving from asking 'did optimization fail to bridge the gap?' to 'does the data's inherent semantic structure make a gap inevitable for certain tasks?'. The resulting framework provides a diagnostic tool to fundamentally attribute failures to data semantics versus optimization artifacts.

ideas.title: Theoretical Framework for Securing Multimodal Training Against Bias-Inducing Attacks
ideas.description: This research develops a formal theoretical framework to analyze how adversarial manipulations in training data—specifically data poisoning attacks—can exploit the structural modality gap in multimodal models to induce or amplify unfair biases. It moves beyond treating security and fairness as separate concerns by modeling the modality gap as a vulnerability surface that can be targeted to cause disparate performance impacts across subgroups. The outcome will be a set of provable defenses and robust training objectives that simultaneously harden models against these bias-inducing poisoning attacks and ensure fairness is preserved even under adversarial data conditions.

ideas.title: Adapting Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) for AI Benchmark Design
ideas.description: This research seed proposes adapting the Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) methodology from systems engineering to the design of AI evaluation benchmarks. In the analogous field, FMEA provides a structured, proactive framework for identifying how a system might fail, analyzing the causes and effects of those failures, and prioritizing them based on risk. Adapted to AI research, this would involve creating a formal process to: 1) systematically brainstorm and catalog potential failure modes of a model (e.g., vulnerability to adversarial prefixes, collapse of reasoning chain under negation, unfair performance disparities across groups); 2) analyze the likely root causes (e.g., data sparsity, architectural limitations, objective function flaws); and 3) rank them by estimated severity, frequency, and difficulty of detection. The output is a prioritized map of failure risks that directly guides the construction of new benchmark tasks and datasets, ensuring evaluation efforts are systematically targeted at the most critical robustness gaps and failure cases, moving beyond anecdotal or convenience-based testing.

ideas.title: Causal Attribution of Societal Impact to Foundational Data Divergence
ideas.description: This research develops a scalable framework to assess the long-term societal impact of foundation models by inverting the causal question. Instead of treating models as black-box optimizers, it tests the hypothesis that fundamental semantic divergences between modalities in the training data (e.g., gaps between text, image, and video representations of concepts) are a primary causal driver of downstream systemic biases and societal effects. The methodology creates causal graphs linking specific data-structure gaps to measurable societal outcomes, enabling scalable, predictive impact audits that can prescribe targeted interventions at the data curation level before model training.

ideas.title: Ecological Data Scaling: Modeling the Minimal Data Ecosystem for Emergent AI Diversity
ideas.description: This research applies ecological principles of niche construction and resource partitioning to the problem of data scaling for emergence. Instead of seeking a single minimal dataset for one capability, it investigates how to structure a minimal, heterogeneous data landscape to trigger and sustain a diverse set of emergent capabilities across a population of models. This involves modeling data as a multi-dimensional resource, where different data 'patches' or sequences foster different model 'species' with specialized skills. The goal is to derive ecological scaling laws that predict the diversity and robustness of an AI model ecosystem as a function of the composition and scale of its foundational data environment.

ideas.title: Investigating Loss Function Topography and Modality Separation via Statistical Physics Analogies
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea by drawing an analogy from statistical physics. The analogous problem is understanding how distinct phases (e.g., liquid and solid) emerge from a homogeneous system, which is solved by analyzing the free energy landscape and its minima under varying conditions. Adapted to multi-modal learning, we propose to treat the training objective's loss function as defining an energy landscape over the joint embedding space. The study will investigate if and how the modality gap emerges with non-contrastive objectives by analyzing the topography of this landscape—specifically, the stability, depth, and separation of minima corresponding to different modalities. This framework allows for a principled comparison of loss functions, predicting which objectives naturally induce separated modality clusters (a 'gap') versus those that promote a more unified, mixed representation, based on the geometric properties of their optimized solutions.

ideas.title: Adapting Phylogenetic Comparative Methods to Characterize the Multi-Modal Embedding Space
ideas.description: The original idea proposes extending the analysis of the modality gap to models with three or more modalities. Drawing an analogy from evolutionary biology, where phylogenetic comparative methods solve the problem of analyzing interrelated traits across many species, we can adapt this framework. In phylogenetics, the shared evolutionary history (a tree) creates covariance that must be modeled to understand true independent adaptation. Similarly, in a multi-modal embedding space, modalities are not independent; their representational structures are interrelated. This research would adapt phylogenetic models (e.g., PGLS) to characterize the high-dimensional modality gap. We would define a 'modality phylogeny' based on feature-space distances or data-generating processes, then use it to decompose the total representational disparity into components attributable to shared modality 'ancestry' versus unique, pairwise divergence. This moves beyond pairwise gap analysis to a systemic understanding of the multi-modal embedding geometry.

ideas.title: Interdisciplinary Governance Frameworks for Multi-Modal Foundation Model Alignment
ideas.description: This research program establishes a deep collaboration between computer scientists, ethicists, legal scholars, and domain experts to investigate how the 'modality gap'—the misalignment in representation spaces—manifests and compounds in foundation models integrating three or more modalities (e.g., text, image, audio, sensor data, biological sequences). The core synergy is to rigorously map these technical alignment failures onto specific societal risks, such as biased decision-making in healthcare or law, and to use this empirical foundation to co-design and evaluate novel governance mechanisms. The outcome is a set of evidence-based, multi-modal-specific frameworks for auditing, regulating, and ethically deploying complex AI systems, ensuring technical diagnostics directly inform robust policy.

ideas.title: Optimal Transport as a Diagnostic and Design Tool for Loss Function-Induced Modality Gaps
ideas.description: This research direction systematically investigates the formation of modality gaps under a spectrum of multimodal loss functions (e.g., CLIP, SLIP, non-contrastive losses) by using optimal transport (OT) theory as a core analytical tool. Instead of merely measuring the final gap, it uses OT metrics like the Wasserstein distance to track the evolution of distributional divergence during training and decompose its geometric causes (e.g., variance collapse, anisotropic stretching). The synergy lies in using these OT-based diagnostics to inform the design of novel loss functions or regularizers that directly optimize for favorable OT properties, such as minimizing transport cost between modalities while preserving semantic structure, thereby bridging the gap from the objective function itself.

ideas.title: Causal Ablation of Loss Function Components on Modality Gap Emergence in Synthetic Multi-Modal Latent Spaces
ideas.description: This research moves beyond observation to establish causality by developing a synthetic data framework where the true data-generating process and optimal latent structure are known. We design a parameterized family of loss functions that decomposes and independently controls key components like alignment strength, uniformity pressure, and negative sample usage. By training models on this controlled synthetic benchmark and analyzing the resulting latent geometries, we can directly attribute specific properties of the modality gap (its existence, width, and density) to individual loss function mechanisms. This provides a foundational causal map for predicting and engineering geometric structure through objective design in real-world multi-modal systems.

ideas.title: Verifiable, Non-Destructive Adaptation via Capability-Specific Skill Modules
ideas.description: This research direction develops techniques to distill the broad, latent capabilities of a large foundation model into a collection of discrete, compact 'skill modules.' Each module is a verifiable, self-contained function representing a specific capability (e.g., logical deduction, stylistic transformation). Adaptation to a new task is achieved not by fine-tuning the base model, but by discovering and composing a sequence of these pre-extracted modules, guided by a lightweight controller. This preserves the integrity of the base model, provides a clear audit trail for model behavior, and enables efficient, interpretable adaptation to diverse downstream requirements.

ideas.title: Adversarial Benchmark Generation Loop for Unbounded Robustness Evaluation
ideas.description: This approach establishes a continuous, two-agent system for evaluation. One agent is the model under test, and the other is a specialized adversary trained to generate inputs that cause failures (e.g., logical contradictions, distributional shifts, or ambiguous instructions). These adversarial probes are validated as legitimate test cases by human raters or a high-reference model, then added to an evolving benchmark. This creates a moving target that systematically uncovers failure modes without allowing the evaluated model to statically overfit, providing a more accurate measure of true robustness.

ideas.title: Adversarial Domain Adaptation with Spectral Filtering for Modality Alignment
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysics, where spectral filtering separates mixed seismic signals from different geological domains, to the problem of modality alignment. The core method involves integrating a spectral decomposition layer (e.g., via learned Fourier or wavelet transforms) into the representation learning pipeline for multiple modalities. Adversarial domain adaptation techniques are then applied not to the entire feature vector, but to specific, learned spectral bands of the representations. This allows the model to explicitly align task-relevant frequency components across modalities (e.g., the shape information in an image and a 3D point cloud) while preserving or even enhancing the separation of modality-specific, nuisance spectral components (e.g., texture in images vs. point density in point clouds). This provides a principled, filter-based mechanism for controlled alignment and separation, moving beyond global feature alignment.

ideas.title: Dynamic Fidelity Adaptation: Closing the Performance Gap in Base Model Preservation
ideas.description: This research develops a dynamic adaptation system that does not assume a one-size-fits-all alternative to fine-tuning. Instead, it introduces a controller network that assesses input complexity and performance requirements in real-time, routing tasks through a hierarchy of adaptation mechanisms of varying expressivity and cost. This allows the system to match or approach full fine-tuning performance on demanding subtasks by selectively deploying more powerful but transient adaptations, while strictly preserving the base model's core parameters and defaulting to ultra-lightweight methods for routine operations. The result is a robust, performance-aware adaptation framework that eliminates the traditional trade-off between model integrity and downstream task capability.

ideas.title: Continuously Adaptive Edge Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research develops principles and a system architecture for foundation models that are not only efficient for edge deployment but are fundamentally designed for continuous, on-device adaptation. The core innovation is a lightweight meta-learning manager that enables a compact core model to safely evolve its specialization in response to changing data, tasks, and user behavior in the open world. This approach overcomes the brittleness of static specialized models by embedding a principled, resource-constrained adaptation loop directly into the edge runtime, ensuring long-term robustness and relevance without compromising efficiency or privacy.

ideas.title: Causal Topological Intervention for Modality Gap Engineering
ideas.description: This research moves beyond passive characterization to actively test the functional role of the modality gap's topology. It first applies TDA to quantify the gap's shape and connectivity across different model architectures and training regimes. It then formulates interventions—such as topological regularization terms that penalize specific homology features (e.g., high-dimensional holes)—designed to alter this structure. Finally, it rigorously evaluates whether these induced topological changes causally improve cross-modal alignment, zero-shot transfer, and adversarial robustness, thereby engineering the gap for better performance.

ideas.title: Orchestrated Specialization: An Interoperability-First Ecosystem for Composable Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research designs an ecosystem where diverse, specialized foundation models are not only incentivized but are technically compelled to be composable. It establishes a minimal, open protocol for model interaction—covering input/output signatures, state passing, and capability discovery—as a non-negotiable standard for participation. The system includes a curated registry and a market mechanism that rewards models both for high performance in their specialization and for demonstrated interoperability within multi-model pipelines. This ensures specialization reduces rather than increases systemic complexity, making a heterogeneous model ecosystem practically usable and economically sustainable.

ideas.title: Adversarial Security Audits: Benchmarks for Training Pipeline Integrity
ideas.description: This research direction develops specialized evaluation benchmarks that treat the training pipeline as a system-under-test. It designs adversarial probes and failure mode analyses specifically targeting security and privacy vulnerabilities, such as data poisoning resilience, membership inference susceptibility, and gradient leakage. These benchmarks provide standardized, quantitative measures of pipeline integrity, enabling comparative security evaluations across different training methods and architectures. The core innovation is shifting robustness evaluation upstream from the model artifact to the training process, creating a feedback loop where security benchmarks directly inform the development of more secure training algorithms.

ideas.title: Causal Evidentiary Audits: Using Legal Standards to Attribute Model Failures to Data Divergence
ideas.description: This research proposes a new model auditing paradigm that treats unexplained model failures as potential 'causal torts' to be investigated. It develops formal procedures, inspired by legal reasoning and evidentiary standards (e.g., preponderance of evidence, differential diagnosis), to systematically test whether a model's failure on a multimodal task is attributable to the fundamental, irreducible semantic divergence between its training data modalities. The audit framework inverts the typical optimization-centric analysis, instead constructing a 'causal case' that isolates data divergence as the primary gap cause, thereby providing a rigorous, attribution-focused diagnostic tool for AI safety and robustness.

ideas.title: Capability Distillation via Lossy Compression and Error-Correcting Codes
ideas.description: This idea solves the inverse problem of distilling broad capabilities into smaller, verifiable modules by drawing an analogy from digital signal processing. The analogous problem is transmitting a high-bandwidth signal (e.g., audio, video) over a limited channel, which is solved using lossy compression paired with error-correcting codes. Compression removes less-critical information, while codes add redundancy to protect essential data. Adapted to AI, we propose a distillation framework that intentionally compresses a large model's capabilities in a lossy but quantifiable manner, identifying and preserving only the most salient parameters for a target capability profile. Concurrently, we design small, verifiable 'capability-correcting' modules that act like codes, encapsulating the minimal relational logic needed to faithfully reconstruct the model's outputs, ensuring the compact system remains provably competent within its defined scope.

ideas.description: This research direction investigates the co-design of foundation model training datasets through deep collaboration between AI researchers, legal scholars, ethicists, and domain experts. Instead of merely curating data for quality, the project develops methodologies to synthesize and document data with embedded legal and ethical provenance—tracking its origins, intended use, and compliance with specific normative frameworks. This enables the creation of specialized, high-impact models (e.g., for healthcare, law, or public policy) whose training data and resulting behaviors can be rigorously audited and validated against interdisciplinary standards before deployment, mitigating downstream risks.

ideas.title: Ecosystemic Incentives for Model Specialization via Niche Construction Theory
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that foundation model development inevitably homogenizes toward a single general-purpose architecture. Drawing an analogy from community ecology, where competitive exclusion is prevented by niche construction and resource partitioning, we propose designing AI ecosystems that technically enable and economically incentivize diverse, specialized foundation models. The analogous ecological solution involves species evolving to exploit specific, often self-created, niches, facilitated by environmental feedback loops. Adapted to AI, this means creating technical standards for 'niche descriptors' (machine-readable specifications of a model's specialized domain, capability, or approach) and coupling them with incentive mechanisms—such as specialized benchmarking, routing protocols, and reward functions—that value unique contributions. The goal is to engineer ecosystemic feedback where developing a distinct specialization is more sustainably profitable than directly competing on broad benchmarks, thus promoting a resilient, diverse model ecosystem instead of a monolithic homogenized one.

ideas.title: A Thermodynamic Stability Framework for Modality Gap Fairness
ideas.description: This research develops a formal theoretical framework, by analogy with statistical mechanics, to explain how the modality gap influences model fairness. Drawing from thermodynamics, where phase stability is analyzed using free energy landscapes and energy barriers, we model the multimodal latent space as a potential landscape. Different data groups (e.g., demographic subgroups) occupy different minima ('phases'), and the modality gap creates barriers or wells that trap representations. The framework will formally define metrics for the 'depth' and 'separation' of group representations relative to the gap, theorize how training dynamics navigate this landscape, and derive the conditions (e.g., required data mixture, architectural interventions) under which the system transitions from a biased metastable state to a fairer global equilibrium. This provides a principled, physics-inspired theory for predicting and mitigating unfairness arising from modality gaps.

ideas.title: Edge Model Metabolic Scaling: Bio-inspired Efficiency Principles for Constrained Devices
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea of developing efficient foundation model principles for edge devices by drawing an analogy from the field of comparative physiology and allometric scaling in biology. The analogous problem—how biological metabolic rates scale efficiently with organism size—was solved through principles like Kleiber's Law, which describes a non-linear (3/4-power) scaling relationship, and through the evolution of fractal-like, space-filling resource distribution networks (e.g., circulatory systems) that optimize for surface area and minimize energy loss relative to size. We adapt this solution to the AI research context by proposing the development of 'Edge Model Metabolic Scaling' principles. Instead of simply compressing large models, we will derive foundational design rules where a model's architectural complexity scales sub-linearly with its target performance and the specific resource constraints (compute, memory, energy) of an edge device class. The core technical innovation involves designing AI model equivalents of biological vascular networks: efficient, fractal-like data and attention pathways that maximize information exchange per computational unit. This bio-inspired approach aims to generate a new class of inherently efficient, right-sized foundation model architectures specifically tailored for the 'metabolic' constraints of edge environments.

ideas.title: Characterizing Modality Gap Topology via Persistent Homology of Phase Space
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from condensed matter physics, where persistent homology is used to characterize the void and pore structure of complex materials. The analogous problem of quantifying the shape and connectivity of empty spaces in a disordered medium is solved by applying topological data analysis to point cloud representations of the material. Adapted to machine learning, we treat the latent or feature space of a multimodal model as the medium. By applying persistent homology to point clouds constructed from data samples near and within the modality gap—the region separating embeddings of different modalities like text and images—we can produce a topological signature of the gap. This signature, comprising persistence diagrams or barcodes, will characterize the gap's connectivity, the presence of isolated pockets or tunnels, and the robustness of these features across scales, providing a richer, shape-based understanding of modality separation than scalar distance measures alone.
