ideas.title: Economic Analysis of Tightly-Coupled AI Modalities as Market Infrastructure
ideas.description: This research investigates the economic and market consequences of eliminating the persistent gap between modalities (e.g., vision, language, audio) in foundation models. By applying econometric models and game theory, it analyzes how a tightly-coupled, fully intermixed multimodal architecture functions as a new form of market infrastructure, potentially reducing transaction costs and information asymmetries in downstream AI service markets. The core question is whether this technical integration alters competitive landscapes by creating different economies of scale, barriers to entry, and innovation incentives compared to loosely-coupled or pipeline-based systems. This provides a novel framework for predicting which technical paradigms will dominate based on the economic ecosystems they engender, not just their benchmark performance.

ideas.title: Control-Theoretic Stabilization of Multi-Modal Latent Dynamics Under Non-Contrastive Losses
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a control-theoretic analysis of the training dynamics of multi-modal models using non-contrastive loss functions (e.g., masked modeling, reconstruction). It models the evolution of the latent representations for each modality as a coupled dynamical system, where the loss function defines the system's vector field. The core investigation is to formally analyze the stability and attractor states of this system to predict if and when a modality gap will emerge as a stable but undesirable equilibrium. The synergy lies in then designing feedback mechanisms—inspired by control theory—to actively steer these dynamics, ensuring the joint embedding space remains stable, aligned, and gap-free throughout training, regardless of the chosen non-contrastive objective.

ideas.title: Dual: Create Small, Interpretable Proxy Models via Circuit Equivalents
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from electrical engineering, where complex, opaque circuits are represented by simple, equivalent circuits that match their external behavior. The analogous problem is understanding a large, black-box foundation model's failure modes. The solution is to build small, interpretable proxy models that act as 'behavioral equivalents' for these failures. Specifically, we develop algorithmic techniques to synthesize a minimal, interpretable model (e.g., a small decision tree, linear model, or tiny neural network) that replicates the input-output failure patterns of a large model within a defined operational domain. This proxy serves as a safe, analyzable, and efficient stand-in for diagnosing root causes of failures, enabling targeted interventions and robustness testing without deploying the massive original model.

ideas.title: Composable Foundation Components: An Architectural and Incentive-Based Alternative to Model Monoculture
ideas.description: This research designs and validates an ecosystem to structurally prevent model homogenization by making it technically obsolete and economically non-competitive. The core technical work develops standards and infrastructure for dynamic, on-the-fly composition of diverse, specialized foundation components—such as modular transformers, diffusers, or reasoning engines—into a coherent system that outperforms monolithic models. Concurrently, it designs economic and governance mechanisms, including component registries, attribution-based reward systems, and benchmark suites that reward compositional diversity, to create sustainable incentives for developing and sharing specialized modules rather than racing to build a single general-purpose giant model.

ideas.title: Topologically-Guided Optimal Transport for Modality Gap Bridging
ideas.description: This research direction fuses topological data analysis (TDA) and optimal transport (OT) to create a principled framework for bridging the modality gap. First, TDA is used to map the high-dimensional shape, connectivity, and homology (e.g., loops, voids) of the low-density region separating modality-specific clusters. This topological signature then directly informs the cost function and constraints of an optimal transport model, ensuring the computed alignment or interpolation paths respect the intrinsic geometric and semantic structure of the gap. The result is a bridging mechanism that not only minimizes distributional distance but also navigates through the representation space in a topologically plausible manner, potentially leading to more coherent cross-modal generation and translation.

ideas.title: Ecology-Inspired Robust Ensembles: Securing AI Ecosystems via Diversity and Redundancy
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel paradigm for securing foundation model training by architecting the training process as a diverse, interacting ecosystem of sub-models or training runs. Inspired by ecological concepts like species diversity, niche partitioning, and trophic cascades, the approach intentionally cultivates a population of models with varied architectures, data subsets, or learning objectives. This intrinsic diversity creates natural redundancy and competitive dynamics that make the overall system inherently more resilient to data poisoning attacks (as poisoned data affects 'species' differently) and privacy leaks (as sensitive information is diffused and obfuscated across the ecosystem). The core innovation is engineering the training ecosystem's 'ecology'—its diversity, interaction rules, and selection pressures—to produce a robust, secure, and private collective intelligence as an emergent property.

ideas.title: Holistic Proactive Immunity for Foundation Model Training Systems
ideas.description: This research designs a system that provides holistic, proactive security for foundation model training by modeling the entire pipeline as a unified dynamical system. It integrates a real-time 'Immunity Monitor' that uses formal verification and machine learning to establish a continuous behavioral baseline, detecting and containing deviations indicative of novel, coordinated data poisoning or privacy attacks. The approach focuses on securing systemic interactions and emergent properties, moving beyond point solutions to ensure robustness against threats that exploit the complexity of the training process itself.

ideas.title: Applying Portfolio Theory to Engineer a Robust and Diverse AI Model Ecosystem
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from Modern Portfolio Theory in financial economics to the challenge of fostering diversity in an AI model ecosystem. In finance, a diversified portfolio manages risk by combining assets whose values don't move in perfect sync. Analogously, we can model an ecosystem of AI models as a portfolio, where each model has an associated 'performance return' and 'failure risk'. The core adapted method is to quantify the 'covariance' or correlation between models' error patterns or weaknesses across a diverse set of inputs or adversarial probes. Using this covariance matrix, we can then apply portfolio optimization algorithms to select or train a collection of models that, when combined (e.g., via ensemble or as a coordinated system), yield the highest possible collective accuracy and robustness for a desired level of 'ecosystem risk'. This shifts the focus from merely maximizing individual model performance to strategically engineering non-correlated failure modes, thereby creating a more resilient and functionally diverse AI ecosystem, much like a financially stable investment portfolio.

ideas.title: Heterogeneity-Aware Stress Testing (HAST): Benchmarks for Measuring and Mitigating Systemic Risks from Model Homogenization
ideas.description: This research develops Heterogeneity-Aware Stress Testing (HAST) benchmarks, a new class of evaluation designed to diagnose systemic risks arising from model homogenization. These benchmarks systematically probe for correlated failure modes across different models and deployments, identifying shared vulnerabilities that could lead to widespread, simultaneous failures. The results directly inform both technical strategies (e.g., promoting architectural or training diversity) and governance frameworks (e.g., regulatory requirements for demonstrated heterogeneity) to build a more resilient AI ecosystem. Thus, evaluation becomes the core engine for measuring and actively managing homogenization risk, moving beyond merely assessing single-model robustness.

ideas.title: Immunological Training: A Self-Auditing Framework for Foundation Model Integrity
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from computational immunology to secure foundation model training. The analogous problem is an organism's defense against pathogens and internal malfunctions, solved through innate/adaptive layers, negative selection, and immune memory. The adapted idea proposes an 'Immunological Training' framework. This involves implementing an 'innate' security layer with rule-based filters and data provenance, and an 'adaptive' layer that uses machine learning to detect novel poisoning patterns and build a memory of attacks. Crucially, a process analogous to negative selection will continuously audit the model's internal representations and updates during training to identify and mitigate the subtle, learned influences of malicious data or privacy leaks, creating a self-auditing, evolving defense system for the training pipeline.

ideas.title: Shared Parameter Architectures as Coupled Oscillators: Mitigating Modality Interference
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the common assumption of independent encoders in multimodal learning by investigating the modality gap in architectures with heavily shared parameters. Drawing an analogy from coupled oscillator systems in physics and engineering, where strong coupling can cause synchronization and loss of individual dynamics, we adapt their mitigation strategies. In those systems, controlled asymmetry or detuning preserves distinct characteristics. Adapted to this context, the research idea is to treat shared encoders as coupled systems and design the shared parameter space with learnable detuning mechanisms—like modality-specific parameter subspaces or controlled regularization. This approach aims to model and manage the interference caused by parameter sharing, preventing representation collapse and enabling the maintenance of essential modality-specific features within a largely shared architecture, thereby relaxing the independence assumption in a principled way.

ideas.title: Dynascope: Adaptive, Interactive Probes for Latent Failure Mode Discovery
ideas.description: This system moves beyond static proxy models by creating an adaptive, interpretable controller that actively interrogates a black-box foundation model. It learns a policy for generating targeted, minimal-input probes to dynamically discover context-sensitive failures like prompt injections or reasoning degeneracies that a static dataset would miss. The controller's decisions and the resulting failure-triggering patterns are both designed to be human-interpretable, creating a live map of the model's failure boundaries. This enables continuous, efficient auditing and root-cause analysis of emergent failures without full model replication.

ideas.title: Theoretical Foundations for Fairness-Aware Model Specialization Ecosystems
ideas.description: This research develops a formal theoretical framework that connects the structural properties of the modality gap in multimodal learning to systemic fairness outcomes. It then uses this framework to derive design principles and incentive mechanisms for ecosystems of specialized foundation models, ensuring that the push for diversity and specialization does not entrench or worsen existing biases. The core novelty is a co-design theory where fairness analysis directly informs the technical and economic rules of a model ecosystem, creating a provable link between architectural choices, market incentives, and equitable performance across groups.

ideas.title: Manifold-Based Adaptive Model Predictive Control for High-Dimensional Model Steering
ideas.description: This research integrates representation learning with adaptive control theory to steer large models. It identifies a stable, low-dimensional manifold of the model's internal dynamics to serve as a tractable state space for control. An online system identification module continuously learns local linear approximations of the dynamics on this manifold, enabling the use of adaptive Model Predictive Control (MPC) to compute precise, optimal interventions. This approach provides formal stability and convergence guarantees for the local linearized system while remaining computationally feasible and responsive to the model's complex, evolving behavior.

Instead of seeking a single minimal scale, it maps the Pareto frontier where scale and curricular design trade off, using a synthetic data generator to create precise, controllable data curricula. The approach quantifies how strategic data ordering and mixture can significantly reduce the total data volume required to elicit a capability, providing a more actionable framework for efficient model development.

ideas.title: Adapting Formal Hardware Verification to Guarantee Neural Network Safety Properties
ideas.description: This research seed proposes adapting the formal property verification (FPV) methodology, a cornerstone of digital hardware design verification, to the domain of machine learning models. In hardware, FPV uses temporal logic specifications (e.g., LTL) to mathematically prove that a circuit design adheres to critical safety and liveness properties before costly fabrication. Analogously, we adapt this solution by formally specifying desired model behaviors—such as fairness constraints ('output must be independent of protected attribute'), robustness bounds ('small input perturbations cause bounded output changes'), or monotonicity rules—as logical properties. We then employ formal methods like model checking or automated theorem proving on a formal abstraction of the neural network to provide mathematical guarantees that these properties hold for all inputs, moving beyond statistical testing to deliver verifiable safety certificates for AI models.

ideas.title: Interactive Specification Refinement for Robust Formal Verification of Model Behaviors
ideas.description: This research develops a framework that treats the formal specification not as a static input, but as a living document co-evolved with the model verification process. The system formally verifies a model against an initial property set, then deploys the model in a simulated or sandboxed environment to monitor for violations of broader, implicitly desired behaviors. Discrepancies and novel failure modes are formalized into candidate new properties, which are presented to a human for validation and integration, closing the specification gap and leading to a more complete and robust verified guarantee.

ideas.title: A Statistical Mechanics Framework for Predicting Phase Transitions in Model Capabilities
ideas.description: This research seed proposes developing a theoretical framework for predicting emergent capabilities in foundation models by drawing an analogy to phase transitions in statistical mechanics. In condensed matter physics, macroscopic properties like magnetism emerge from microscopic interactions, and statistical mechanics uses order parameters and scaling laws to predict phase transitions at critical thresholds. Adapting this solution, the research will define quantitative 'capability order parameters' for AI models (e.g., metrics for reasoning or generalization) and seek scaling laws that describe their behavior as a function of scale parameters (model size, dataset size, compute). The resulting framework aims to predict sharp transitions or the emergence of new capabilities at specific scaling thresholds, providing a principled theory for capability forecasting in AI.

ideas.title: Phase Transitions in Data: Identifying Critical Dataset Sizes for Emergent Capabilities
ideas.description: This research seed adapts the solution from a key problem in statistical physics—finding the critical threshold for emergence in percolation networks—to the study of AI scaling. The analogous problem is determining the minimal density of connections needed for a giant component to suddenly appear in a random graph. The analogous solution involves parameter sweeps and finite-size scaling analysis to pinpoint this phase transition. Adapted to machine learning, the idea is to investigate the dual problem of minimal data scale by treating a dataset as a network where data points or skills are nodes. By constructing datasets of controlled size and conceptual interconnectedness, training models, and applying percolation-inspired scaling analysis, the research aims to identify the critical dataset size and mixture properties required to trigger specific emergent capabilities, moving beyond simple parameter counting to understand the data's structural role in capability emergence.

ideas.title: Applying Biodiversity Conservation Strategies to Foundation Model Ecosystems
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from ecology and biodiversity conservation to address the risk of homogenization from single foundation models. The analogous problem is agricultural or ecological monoculture, which creates systemic vulnerability to pests, disease, or environmental change. Conservation biology solves this through technical strategies like seed banks and genetic archives, combined with governance policies that protect diverse habitats and incentivize polyculture. Adapted to AI, the research idea involves developing two parallel strategies: 1) Technical 'Model Seed Banks'—infrastructure and methods to archive, curate, and enable the continued development of a wide variety of model architectures, training datasets, and specialized capabilities, ensuring diverse 'genetic' lines remain available. 2) Governance 'Diversity Incentives'—policy and economic frameworks, such as procurement rules, interoperability standards, and regulatory sandboxes, that reward organizations for maintaining and deploying multiple, distinct foundation models rather than standardizing on one. The combined goal is to create a resilient, heterogeneous AI ecosystem less prone to systemic failure, bias amplification, or innovation stagnation.

ideas.title: Adversarial Audit Simulation with Formalized Burdens of Proof for Model Accountability
ideas.description: This research designs a computational framework that simulates an adversarial legal process to audit model decisions. It instantiates opposing audit agents—one seeking to establish harm or error, another to defend the model—within a structured environment that formalizes burdens of proof and rules of evidence. The system generates and evaluates counterfactual scenarios and argumentative chains, producing an audit report that includes a dynamic 'reasoning trace' and a quantified verdict on the decision's robustness, thereby embedding the dialectical core of legal reasoning into the audit mechanism itself.

ideas.title: Decentralized Model Ecosystems: Technical and Governance Frameworks for Pluralistic AI
ideas.description: This research proposes the design of decentralized, participatory ecosystems for foundation model development where technical protocols and governance structures are co-designed to actively foster and maintain model pluralism. It moves beyond simply distributing control to architecting systems—through mechanisms like differentiated training data flows, incentive-aligned compute markets, and composable modular architectures—that encourage the emergence of a diverse population of models with varied capabilities, values, and specializations. The core innovation is treating diversity as a first-order system property, ensuring the ecosystem itself is resilient to monoculture risks and centralized points of failure, thereby achieving both robust decentralization and intrinsic heterogeneity.

ideas.description: This research develops a methodology that combines large-scale data analytics with structured multi-stakeholder deliberation to map the systemic societal impacts of foundation models. It creates scalable digital platforms for gathering diverse impact data while embedding participatory workshops and value-sensitive design protocols to define, weight, and contest impact metrics. The output is not a single assessment score but a dynamic 'impact map' that visualizes trade-offs, value conflicts, and distributional effects across different communities and time horizons. This approach ensures assessments are both scalable and grounded in the pluralistic, often conflicting, values of affected societies.

It develops methods to identify and isolate coherent sub-computations within a large model that are responsible for specific capabilities. These sub-computations are then distilled into compact modules that are accompanied by formal specifications and proofs of causal alignment with the source. The final output is a verifiably faithful module whose reliability and failure modes are rigorously understood, ensuring robustness beyond mere input-output mimicry.

ideas.title: Establish a 'Technology Impact Assessment' Protocol for Foundation Models
ideas.description: This idea proposes to solve the challenge of studying foundation models' legal and ethical impact by adapting a proven solution from environmental science: the Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) process. In that field, deep, structured collaboration across disciplines (ecology, sociology, economics, etc.) is mandated to predict and mitigate the consequences of major projects before they proceed. We adapt this by establishing a formal 'Technology Impact Assessment' (TIA) protocol. For any major foundation model deployment, developers would be required to convene a standing, interdisciplinary panel—integrating expertise from AI research, law, ethics, sociology, and economics—to collaboratively research, forecast, and document the model's potential societal impacts in a public report. This TIA would serve as a critical, evidence-based foundation for regulatory decision-making, ongoing model governance, and public accountability, thereby institutionalizing deep interdisciplinary collaboration for concrete societal impact.

ideas.title: Federated Specialization Ecosystems: Incentivizing Modular, Small-Scale Models for Collective Capability
ideas.description: This research direction proposes designing a federated ecosystem where the primary units are not monolithic models but specialized, smaller-scale foundation models. The core challenge is to create technical protocols and incentive mechanisms—such as specialized compute markets, verifiable contribution records, and dynamic routing systems—that encourage the development and maintenance of diverse model specializations. These modular components would then be composed on-demand through a federated network, where a query is dynamically decomposed and routed to the most competent specialized model(s), with results synthesized. This approach challenges both homogenization and massive scale by making collective, emergent capability the product of a sustainable, incentivized economy of specialization rather than a single centralized model.

ideas.title: A Causal and Predictive Theory of Emergence via Controlled Scaling Experiments and Mechanistic Probes
ideas.description: This research program develops a predictive, causal theory of emergent capabilities by tightly coupling controlled experimentation across model scales with mechanistic interpretability. Instead of merely describing observed emergence, it designs targeted, multi-scale experiments on simplified model families and data regimes to isolate and test hypothesized drivers of specific capabilities. The output is a suite of falsifiable, mechanistic models that predict the scaling thresholds and functional forms of new capabilities, validated against both controlled experiments and large-scale foundation models, ultimately providing actionable guidance for capability forecasting and model development.

ideas.title: Adaptive Sentinel Indicators for Systemic AI Impact
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from ecosystem ecology, this research adapts the concept of 'sentinel indicators' to create a scalable methodology for assessing the long-term, systemic societal impact of foundation models. In ecology, scientists monitor vast, complex ecosystems by tracking a minimal set of key species or abiotic metrics that serve as reliable proxies for overall system health. Similarly, this project will develop and validate a core framework of high-leverage societal indicators—such as measurable shifts in information ecosystems, economic opportunity distribution, or institutional trust—that act as early-warning signals for broader systemic effects. The outcome will be a scalable assessment toolkit that moves away from exhaustive, post-hoc studies toward tractable, continuous monitoring of foundational societal variables impacted by AI, enabling more responsive governance and development practices.

ideas.title: Apply Lyapunov Stability Analysis from Robotics to Ensure Model Alignment
ideas.description: This idea adapts a core solution from robotics and nonlinear control—Lyapunov stability analysis—to the problem of AI model alignment and stability. In robotics, Lyapunov theory provides a formal, mathematical framework to prove a complex, nonlinear system will converge to a desired state and remain there despite disturbances, which is analogous to needing a model to stay aligned despite novel prompts or distribution shifts. The adapted research idea involves defining a candidate Lyapunov function for a model's behavior, quantifying its 'distance' from a target aligned manifold. The training process or a lightweight control module (e.g., a steering network) is then designed to act as the controller that ensures the Lyapunov function's derivative is negative, providing a formal certificate of stable, convergent alignment dynamics rather than relying solely on empirical loss minimization.

ideas.title: Adapting Forensic Engineering Failure Analysis for AI Model Auditing
ideas.description: This idea adapts the problem-solving methodology from forensic engineering—specifically its rigorous protocols for investigating structural failures—to the context of auditing AI model decisions. In forensic engineering, when a bridge collapses or a machine fails, investigators follow a standardized process to collect evidence, apply engineering principles, and determine root causes, producing findings that can withstand legal scrutiny. The adapted research idea proposes to develop a model auditing framework that treats problematic model outputs (e.g., discriminatory decisions, critical errors) as analogous to engineering failures. The framework would provide auditors with a step-by-step protocol to systematically reconstruct the decision event, analyze the 'materials' (training data), 'design' (model architecture and objectives), and 'operational context' (input data and deployment environment). The outcome is a structured audit report that establishes a clear, defensible chain of causation—distinguishing between primary faults and contributing factors—thereby incorporating legal reasoning and evidentiary standards directly into the technical audit process.

ideas.title: Decentralized Foundation Model Development via Federated Commons Governance
ideas.description: This research seed proposes inverting the centralization of model development and control by drawing an analogy from ecology and environmental resource management, specifically the governance of common-pool resources. The analogous problem—how to manage a shared, finite resource without centralized authority or tragic overuse—was solved through Elinor Ostrom's empirically derived design principles for successful commons governance. These principles outline how communities self-organize with clear boundaries, participatory rule-making, monitoring, and nested governance. Adapted to AI, the research would investigate how to structure decentralized, open, and participatory frameworks for foundation models by treating key resources (data, compute, model access) as a digital commons. The adapted idea is to design and prototype governance systems based on Ostrom's principles, creating polycentric, community-driven structures for model development, deployment, and ongoing stewardship that ensure sustainability, equity, and accountability without relying on a single centralized entity.

ideas.description: This research develops a dynamic, simulation-driven framework to analyze incentives and market structures in foundation models, explicitly designed for environments with non-stationary dynamics and data scarcity. It combines agent-based modeling, mechanism design, and a continuously updated 'digital twin' of the AI ecosystem that assimilates real-time technical and economic data. The approach focuses on stress-testing governance proposals and market interventions through counterfactual simulation, providing robust, adaptive insights that traditional econometric models cannot capture in this novel and rapidly evolving domain.

ideas.description: This research explores a paradigm where foundation model capabilities are not grown in a single data center but emerge from a federated network of specialized, smaller-scale modules. Each module is developed, hosted, and maintained by diverse, independent participants (individuals, institutions, communities) within an open, decentralized framework. The key innovation is a participatory governance and incentive system that technically coordinates these modules into a coherent collective intelligence, aligning contribution, control, and capability. This inverts both technical and organizational centralization, creating a resilient, adaptable, and democratically governed alternative to monolithic AI.

ideas.description: This research program structures interdisciplinary collaboration around the concrete co-creation of implementable governance artifacts, such as standard contractual clauses for AI procurement, operationalizable fairness audits, or model cards with legal compliance appendices. Each artifact is developed by a core team spanning law, ethics, and computer science, then subjected to iterative, adversarial review by external practitioners who must attempt to use or circumvent it. The final output is a validated toolkit of tested artifacts, accompanied by documented failure modes and implementation guidelines, ensuring research directly translates into societal impact through usable legal and technical instruments.

ideas.title: Adapting Epidemiological Compartmental Models to Analyze AI Model Proliferation and Market Saturation
ideas.description: This idea proposes adapting epidemiological compartmental models, specifically the SIR (Susceptible-Infected-Recovered) framework, to analyze the market dynamics and incentives surrounding foundation models. In epidemiology, these models simulate disease spread through populations using rates of transmission and recovery. Here, we transpose this to model the spread of AI model capabilities through the commercial ecosystem. Firms are categorized into compartments: Susceptible (potential adopters), Infected (active deployers), and Recovered (those for whom the technology is obsolete). The 'transmission' rate is parameterized by factors like model accessibility (API pricing, open-source), competitive pressure, and developer talent flow, while the 'recovery' rate is driven by technological displacement. By simulating this dynamic system, we can study questions of market saturation, the tipping points for monopoly formation, and how different incentive structures—such as tiered licensing or strategic open-sourcing—act as 'public health interventions' to either accelerate or curb the spread of specific model capabilities, providing a novel quantitative lens for market analysis.
